Because every controller event (loadingChanged, selectionChanged, paginationChanged, folderLoaded, etc.) triggers render() (line 302), any background update after the user has clicked an item will yank focus back to that item, stealing focus from wherever the user has since moved on the page. Focus restoration should be gated on whether focus was actually inside the component.

A single item click fires two async controller mutations back-to-back — toggleSelection(itemId) then activateItem(itemId) (lines 665-666) — conflating selection and activation. Each emits its own event, causing multiple full innerHTML re-renders per click, and in 'multiple' selection mode every activation also toggles selection, which is almost certainly unintended UX.

The listbox contains non-option interactive descendants. Item-action <button>s (lines 368-383) sit inside <li> elements within the role="listbox" <ul> and receive the default tabindex 0, so they are tab-focusable children of a listbox — a violation of the listbox pattern, where the only focusable/exposed descendants should be role="option". This breaks assistive-tech navigation and the roving-tabindex model.

selection-mode is not reflected into ARIA. The <ul role="listbox"> (line 641) never sets aria-multiselectable even when selectionMode === 'multiple', so screen readers announce single-select semantics while multiple aria-selected options exist — an inconsistent, confusing state.

Errors are not announced. The error node

has no role="alert"/aria-live, and the only live region (part="status", aria-live="polite") reports 'ready' rather than the error (statusText only distinguishes idle/loading/ready, lines 420-424). A load failure is therefore silent to screen-reader users.

No :active/pressed states are defined for any control (breadcrumb, refresh, load-more, item, item-action). Blueprint buttons have a distinct pressed treatment (the tokens even expose SurfaceSurfaceBrandPressed), so the components feel unresponsive on click compared to Box production controls.

Disabled styling is applied only to the refresh button ([part="refresh"]:disabled). The load-more and item-action buttons get no disabled state and, crucially, are never disabled during loading, so load-more can be clicked repeatedly to double-fire pagination (loadNextPage) before the in-flight request resolves.

attributeChangedCallback() ignores its name/old/new arguments (line 175) and always calls scheduleStart(), which tears down the controller and reconnects for any observed attribute change — including selection-mode and language, which merely need a re-render. This discards fetched state and re-issues the initial folder load on a purely presentational change.

Spacing and radii are hardcoded ad-hoc rem values with no consistent Blueprint rhythm: gaps/padding of 0.85/1.1/0.35/0.2rem and radii of 0.95/0.8/0.7/0.55rem are not aligned to a 4/8px scale and no spacing or radius tokens are used (only color tokens are). Button padding also mixes em (0.45em 1em) with rem radii, producing inconsistent geometry across controls.

No font-family token is applied — :host uses font: inherit and the design system's FontFamilyBase (InterVariable/Lato) is never referenced, so typography depends entirely on the host page rather than the Box Blueprint type stack.

summary: A tidy, token-aware option row with correct event/reflection plumbing, but undermined by a full-innerHTML re-render that destroys focus on any attribute change and a roving-tabindex model that can't actually work because the focusable element is locked inside shadow DOM. Selected/thumb backgrounds also break in dark theme.

issues.description: render() reassigns shadowRoot.innerHTML on every attributeChangedCallback (lines 80-82, 97-189). Toggling any observed attribute — e.g. the host setting `selected` after activation — replaces the inner [part=item] div and destroys focus on it, so keyboard selection inside a listbox loses focus. It also re-parses the full <style> block and re-binds listeners on every attribute change during upgrade and thereafter.

tabindex is hardcoded on the inner shadow-DOM [part=item] div (line 181, always '0' when enabled). The docstring (lines 14-16) claims the host owns roving focus, but a host can only set tabindex on the light-DOM custom element, which is ignored here. So every option is simultaneously tab-focusable and the host cannot implement a single-tab-stop roving-tabindex listbox — breaking the stated interaction model.

Selected background (line 129) and thumb background (line 149) use color-mix(... , white 90%/18%) with a hardcoded literal `white`. In the dark theme this produces a near-white fill on a dark surface, so selected/thumb do not re-theme. A dedicated token --boe-token-surface-item-surface-selected (SurfaceItemSurfaceSelected, #14335a in dark) exists specifically for this and should be used instead of mixing surface-brand with literal white.

No :active / pressed state is implemented on [part=item], despite a dedicated --boe-token-surface-surface-brand-pressed token being available. Hover, focus-visible, selected and disabled are covered, but click/press gives no visual feedback.

The dispatched event is named 'select' (line 89), which collides with the native DOM 'select' event name. Because it is bubbles+composed, an ancestor listening for native select semantics could receive these. A namespaced name (e.g. 'boe-select') would be safer and matches the design-system prefix convention.

The `icon` attribute is only rendered as an escaped text glyph inside the thumb (lines 102, 183); it cannot render an actual icon token/SVG from the design system's icon registry. An empty icon still renders an empty bordered thumb square rather than collapsing or falling back to a label initial.

Text color fallback is a hardcoded #222222 (line 120) rather than a neutral aligned with the Box text token; the item also uses ad-hoc rem spacing values (0.7rem gap, 0.5/0.65rem padding, 0.6rem radius) instead of a shared spacing/radius scale, so rhythm can drift from sibling components.

summary: A polished-looking but shallow date field: it uses the correct --boe-token-* variables and has a clean hover/focus/disabled treatment, but it is not form-associated, never reflects user input back to the value attribute, and has no error/required/validation states despite being a form control.

issues.description: Not a form-associated custom element: no `static formAssociated = true`, no ElementInternals, and no `name` attribute. A `<box-date-field>` inside a <form> contributes nothing on submit and cannot participate in native form validation/reset — a serious gap for a component named 'field'.

Stale attribute reflection on user input: the input listener calls syncValue(), which updates `valueInternal` and dispatches 'value-changed' but never calls setAttribute('value', ...). After a user picks a date, the `value` getter is correct but getAttribute('value') and the reflected DOM attribute remain the old value, breaking the reflection convention every other form component follows.

No error/invalid state at all: there is no `error`/`invalid` attribute, no aria-invalid, no error-message slot/part, and min/max out-of-range values produce no visual feedback. Sibling form components expose an error state (docs even show an 'Error' variant elsewhere); this field has only hover/focus/disabled.

Only a non-standard 'value-changed' CustomEvent is dispatched; no native-style 'change' or 'input' event is emitted and the underlying input's events are swallowed by the shadow boundary (composed input events don't cross), so standard form listeners can't observe changes.

No support for `required` (no required attribute, aria-required, or visual indicator) and no way to associate helper/error text via aria-describedby. Label association works only via the wrapping <label>, but there is no id/for wiring and no aria-label fallback path.

render() rewrites the entire shadowRoot.innerHTML (including the full <style> block) on every attribute change, re-parsing CSS each time and destroying the input node. If a consumer sets .value while the input is focused, focus and any in-progress edit are lost. A cached template / targeted attribute updates would be more robust.

Double render on value set: the `value` setter calls setAttribute('value', ...) (which triggers attributeChangedCallback → render) and then calls render() again directly, causing two full shadow-DOM rebuilds per programmatic value assignment.

No :active/pressed state and no distinct focus treatment for the disabled+hover combination; the picker-open state relies entirely on native browser chrome, which is inconsistent with the custom Blueprint styling of the field itself.

Spacing and radius use ad-hoc rem literals (0.45rem gap, 0.6rem/0.85rem padding, 0.7rem radius) rather than shared spacing/radius tokens, so rhythm can drift from other Blueprint form controls; the native calendar/picker icon is also left unstyled and won't match the Box accent in all browsers.

summary: A visually decent modal that wires up role/aria-modal/aria-labelledby and confirm/cancel events, but it has no focus management or focus trap, a broken Escape handler, and skips hover/active/loading states despite the tokens existing. The docs example even passes an attribute the component ignores.

issues.description: No focus management: when the dialog opens, focus is never moved into it (no autofocus on confirm/first focusable), there is no focus trap (Tab can escape to the inert-but-not-actually-inert background), and focus is not restored to the trigger element on close. aria-modal="true" is asserted but not honored behaviorally.

Escape handling is effectively broken: the keydown listener is attached to the [part="dialog"] <section> (lines 222-229), so it only fires when focus is already inside the dialog. Since focus is never moved into the dialog on open, pressing Escape does nothing in the common case. Escape should be handled at the document/host level while open.

Interactive states are missing. Only :focus-visible is styled (lines 184-188). The confirm button never uses the available --boe-token-surface-surface-brand-hover (#0057c0) / -brand-pressed (#004eaa) tokens for :hover/:active, the cancel button has no hover state, and there is no disabled or loading state for the confirm action (async confirmation is a common dialog pattern).

Docs/component mismatch: the docs example (examples.ts line 405) is <box-dialog title="Delete file?" message="..." open>, but the component only observes/renders `description`, not `message` (observedAttributes line 13). The body text in the documented example never renders — the supporting copy is silently dropped.

The description paragraph is rendered without an id and the dialog has no aria-describedby (lines 191-199). Screen readers announce the title but not the supporting description as the dialog's description.

Hardcoded, non-tokenized colors that won't adapt to dark theme: the backdrop rgba(15,23,42,0.34) and blur (line 110), the box-shadow rgba values including the inset white highlight rgba(255,255,255,0.82) (lines 127-129), and `color: white` on confirm (line 181) instead of the existing --boe-token-text-text-on-brand token. box-dark.ts tokens are ignored for these surfaces.

Background is not made inert when the dialog is open. There is no `inert`/aria-hidden applied to sibling page content and no native top-layer <dialog>/showModal usage, so assistive tech and Tab order can still reach content behind the modal. Body scroll is also not locked.

Every render() rebuilds shadowRoot.innerHTML from scratch (line 99). Any attribute change while open (e.g. updating the title) destroys and recreates all nodes, discarding focus within the dialog and re-attaching all listeners. Combined with the missing focus management this makes mid-open updates hostile.

Inconsistent close semantics: the cancel button and Escape dispatch a `cancel` CustomEvent before closing (lines 214-217, 226-227), but a backdrop click (lines 209-213) calls close() without dispatching `cancel`. Consumers listening for cancel won't hear dismissals via backdrop click.

The `title` property/attribute (lines 45-51) collides with the global HTML `title` attribute, which produces a native browser tooltip when hovering the host element and can confuse assistive tech. A dedicated attribute (e.g. `heading`) or non-reflected property would avoid the collision.

Double render on open: set open (line 27) writes the `open` attribute, which triggers attributeChangedCallback -> render() (lines 74-78), then set open itself dispatches the event and calls render() again (lines 41-42). The open-changed event is also dispatched after the first render, so listeners can't preventDefault/inspect pre-render state.

Arbitrary geometry not clearly mapped to Blueprint scale: border-radius 1.35rem on the dialog and 999px pill buttons, plus ad-hoc gaps (1rem/0.65rem) and padding (0.35rem 0.75rem). These bespoke values sidestep any spacing/radius token rhythm.

Spacing and typography use hardcoded magic numbers rather than any documented scale: gap:0.75rem, font-size:0.72rem (oddly specific, off any 2/4px rhythm), letter-spacing:0.08em, min-block-size:1rem. Only the two colors are tokenized (correctly, via --boe-token-stroke-stroke and --boe-token-text-text-secondary with fallbacks matching the real #e8e8e8/#6f6f6f). The DS ships no spacing/type tokens, so this is partly excusable, but the label styling in particular reads as ad-hoc rather than Blueprint rhythm.

When a label is present the visible <span part="label"> text is left un-aria-hidden while an aria-label is also placed on the role=separator container. Correct output relies entirely on separator name-from-author-id suppressing the child text; it would be more robust to aria-hidden the visible label span. Additionally aria-orientation="horizontal" is redundant (it is the default for role=separator) — harmless noise.

The orientation setter performs no validation: assigning an unknown value (e.g. el.orientation = 'diagonal') writes it verbatim to the attribute, while the getter/CSS normalize to horizontal — so the reflected attribute and the effective orientation silently disagree. The reflection convention (getter/setter + observedAttributes) is otherwise correctly implemented.

render() rebuilds the entire shadowRoot.innerHTML on every attributeChangedCallback, re-parsing the markup and re-injecting the full <style> block each time an attribute changes. A cached <template> or adoptedStyleSheets (styles are static) would avoid the per-change reparse. Minor for a divider but a poor pattern to propagate.

A label set on a vertical divider is silently discarded: hasLabel is forced false for orientation=='vertical', so <box-divider orientation="vertical" label="x"> renders a bare line with no label and no warning. The behavior is intentional (commented) but produces a surprising silent no-op for a consumer setting both.

summary: A clean, correct, XSS-safe divider that correctly tokenizes its two colors and gets the role=separator / aria-orientation semantics right; as a non-interactive component it has no applicable states to miss. Points come off for magic-number (untokenized) spacing/typography, a full innerHTML rebuild per attribute change, unvalidated orientation setter, and silently dropping labels on vertical dividers.

A visually laid-out donut pattern that leans on tokens for text/surface/stroke but hardcodes all segment fill colors, so the shipped docs example renders three identical brand-blue arcs. It also has a degenerate single-segment path bug, misuses list ARIA roles on buttons, and implements almost none of the expected interactive states.

issues.description: Segment colors are chosen solely by an optional `tone` via toneColor() (lines 146-157). The docs-site example (examples.ts 745-751) supplies three segments with NO tone, so every segment falls through to the default '#0061d5' and the donut renders as a single indistinguishable solid-blue ring — the chart cannot communicate its own data out of the box.

toneColor() hardcodes hex literals (#5a7cf7, #26c281, #f59e0b, #0061d5) instead of --boe-token-* variables, and the values don't even match the token palette (accent should be #5b4b8a per SurfaceStatusSurfaceAccent, warning should be #f5b31b per SurfaceStatusSurfaceInprogress). Segment fills and legend swatches therefore ignore the design system and won't adapt to the dark theme in box-dark.ts.

describeSegmentPath() is degenerate for a full-circle segment. A single positive segment (or one at 100%) yields start=0/end=360, so outerStart equals outerEnd and the SVG arc collapses to a zero-length path that renders nothing — the donut disappears whenever there is exactly one segment or one dominant value.

Legend items are <button> elements but are given role="listitem" (lines 275-282), which overrides their implicit button role. Screen readers announce them as plain list items, not activatable controls, hiding the click-to-select affordance. Use a real list of buttons (e.g. <li> wrappers) instead of overriding the button role.

The chart <svg role="img"> aria-label is only '<title> segment distribution' (line 250) with no numeric data, and the individual segment <path> elements are not focusable or keyboard-operable and carry no labels. Non-legend users get no data from the visual; segment selection is reachable only through the legend.

No selected state is implemented even though a 'segment-selected' event is dispatched — clicking a legend item emits the event but nothing in the DOM (segment path highlight, legend aria-pressed/aria-current, active styling) reflects the selection. There are also no disabled, loading, or error states anywhere on the component.

Action buttons ([part="action"], lines 367-382) have no :hover, :active, or :focus-visible styling — no brand focus ring. Only legend items get hover/focus-visible treatment; the SVG segments get no hover feedback either, so the interactive affordances are inconsistent.

The component overrides the native `title` global attribute/property: observedAttributes includes 'title', the getter returns a 'Donut Chart' default, and the setter reflects it (lines 33, 97-103). This collides with the built-in HTMLElement.title tooltip semantics — setting the chart title also produces a browser tooltip on the host and fights the platform property. A dedicated attribute like 'heading'/'chart-title' would be safer.

render() rebuilds shadowRoot.innerHTML wholesale on every attributeChangedCallback and re-binds all listeners; any focus on a legend/action button is lost on re-render, and there is no de-duplication of updates. The empty state also leaks the internal id string ('No donut-chart data available.', line 295) rather than a user-facing message.

focus-visible on legend items uses a token-mixed outline (line 467-470) but the [part="action"] focus ring is entirely absent, so keyboard focus visibility is inconsistent across the two interactive control types in the same component.

summary: A functional reorderable list with clean token usage and a sensible property/event API, but keyboard reordering is only reachable for the first (or last-moved) item, and it lacks hover/disabled/selected states plus a live-region announcement, so it falls short of production accessibility and polish.

issues.description: Roving tabindex (line 120) sets tabindex=0 only on the item matching focusValue ?? items[0] and -1 on all others, but ArrowUp/Down/Home/End MOVE the focused item rather than navigate. A keyboard-only user can therefore only ever focus the first handle (or the last one they moved); there is no way to Tab/arrow to item 3's handle to reorder it. Add arrow-key navigation or a separate move gesture so every item is keyboard-reachable.

focusValue is set on every keyboard move (line 81) and never cleared. Since render() unconditionally calls handle.focus() whenever focusValue is truthy (line 206), any external re-render (host updating the items attribute fires attributeChangedCallback -> render) steals focus back to the stale moved handle, hijacking the user's focus. Reset focusValue after applying focus, or only honor it immediately after a move.

No hover state on rows or the handle. The design system exposes --boe-token-surface-surface-hover / --boe-token-stroke-stroke-hover (used ~25/23 times across other components) but [part=item] and [part=handle] have no :hover rule, so rows give no pointer feedback.

Reorders are not announced via an aria-live region. Announcement relies solely on focus landing on the moved handle so its updated aria-label ('position X of Y') is re-read; drag-and-drop reorders, which do not move focus, are silent to screen readers. Add a visually-hidden aria-live=polite status node reporting the new position.

No disabled state. There is no disabled attribute/reflection in observedAttributes and no :host([disabled]) styling to suppress dragging/keyboard moves and dim the UI, so it cannot be presented as read-only/inactive.

Spacing/radius are arbitrary magic rem values off the Blueprint rhythm: list gap 0.4rem, row padding 0.55rem/0.7rem (8.8px/11.2px), item gap 0.65rem, border-radius 0.7rem/0.4rem. These do not align to a 4/8px grid or documented spacing tokens.

The empty state ([part=empty] 'No items loaded', line 200) has no styling at all: no token color, padding, or muted text-secondary treatment, so it renders as unstyled default text inconsistent with the component.
Handle uses cursor: grab but never switches to grabbing during an active drag (no :active or [data-dragging] cursor rule), so the drag affordance is incomplete.

The focus-visible ring is a soft box-shadow at 22% opacity of the brand color, applied only to the handle rather than a solid brand focus ring; it can read as low-contrast against light surfaces versus a standard 2-3px solid brand ring.

summary: A portaling drawer that references some --boe-token-* variables and sets basic dialog ARIA, but ships a broken Escape handler, no focus management/trap, thin interactive states, and an off-brand glossy aesthetic with many hardcoded colors and off-scale spacing/radii.

issues.description: No focus management on open: when the drawer renders, focus is never moved into the dialog. Combined with aria-modal, keyboard users are left behind the modal with no way in, and focus is never restored to the trigger on close.

No focus trap. Because focus stays in the page behind the drawer, Tab/Shift+Tab cycle through the background content that sits underneath the aria-modal overlay. Background is also never set inert or aria-hidden, so screen readers can reach it.

Escape is effectively dead. The keydown listener is attached to the [part="drawer"] <aside> (line 277), but nothing inside is ever focused, so the aside/its children never receive keydown and Escape cannot close the drawer. The handler should be on document/window, gated by open.

Inconsistent event dispatch. Opening/closing via the open property fires 'open-changed' (line 50), but toggling via the observed 'open' attribute (attributeChangedCallback, lines 77-88) mutates openValue and re-renders without ever dispatching 'open-changed'. Consumers listening for the event miss attribute-driven changes.

Dismiss semantics are inconsistent: the close button (line 273) and Escape (line 281) dispatch a 'dismiss' event, but backdrop click (lines 268-272) calls close() without dispatching 'dismiss', so identical user intent produces different events.

Interactive states are missing on the close button: only :focus-visible is styled (lines 242-245). There is no :hover or :active feedback, so the only actionable control in the component feels static.

Many hardcoded, non-tokenized colors instead of --boe-token-* values: backdrop rgba(15,23,42,0.24) (line 157), drop shadow rgba(15,23,42,0.14) and inset white rgba(255,255,255,0.82) (lines 175-176, 239). Backdrop scrim and elevation shadows should come from tokens.

Spacing and radii do not follow a Blueprint scale: arbitrary values like 1.35rem/1.1rem/0.35rem padding (lines 207, 214) and radii 1.35rem/0.9rem (lines 182, 232, 237). No radius/spacing tokens are used, so rhythm is off-system.

The glossy look (gradient surface via color-mix plus an inset white top highlight, lines 166-176, 239) reads as skeuomorphic rather than Box's flat, cool-neutral Blueprint aesthetic. The Box blue accent appears only as a low-opacity focus outline (line 243).

Overriding the native title property (lines 62-68) shadows the standard HTMLElement.title global attribute and returns 'Drawer' by default, which is surprising/brittle for consumers and tooling that read element.title.
The description paragraph (line 137) is not associated with the dialog via aria-describedby, so assistive tech announces only the title, not the supporting description.

No body scroll lock while open — the underlying page scrolls behind the modal drawer. Also, restoreFromPortal silently no-ops if the original parent was removed, leaving the element stranded on <body>.

Redundant work on close: the open setter removes the attribute (firing attributeChangedCallback -> restoreFromPortal + render), then close() calls restoreFromPortal again and the setter also calls render(), producing multiple render/restore passes per close.

summary: A visually polished drop zone with good token usage and a clean dragging state, but it lacks a visible focus ring, hover/disabled/error/loading states, proper ARIA/live announcements, and it re-renders its own DOM mid-drag which is brittle.

issues.description: No visible focus indicator for keyboard users. The <label part="zone"> is not focusable, and the only focusable element is the file input which is hidden (opacity:0, 1px, pointer-events:none). A keyboard user tabbing to it sees no focus ring on the zone, so the component is effectively unusable/invisible via keyboard. There is no :focus-visible / :focus-within brand focus-ring style on [part="zone"].

Only the dragging state is implemented. There is no :hover state on the zone (despite cursor:pointer), no active state, no disabled state (no `disabled` attribute at all), no error state, no loading/uploading state, and no selected/has-files state. For a production upload control at minimum hover, focus-visible, disabled, and error are expected.

render() rewrites shadowRoot.innerHTML and re-attaches all listeners, and it is called from inside the drag handlers themselves (dragenter -> this.dragging=true; this.render()). Tearing down and rebuilding the drop-target DOM node during an active drag is brittle: the element currently receiving drag events is destroyed, which can trigger spurious dragleave/dragenter cycles, cause flicker, and risk the drop not landing on the intended node. State-driven re-render should update a class/attribute (e.g. toggle data-dragging) instead of replacing innerHTML.

No role, no aria-describedby, and no aria-live region. File selection via drop or input change fires an event but nothing is announced to assistive tech, and the zone exposes no accessible description linking the label/message. The hidden input also lacks an explicit aria-label; its accessible name relies solely on wrapped label text.

The API is missing key upload attributes: `accept` (file-type filtering) is not exposed, `multiple` is hardcoded in the template with no attribute to control it, and there is no `disabled` attribute reflected in observedAttributes. observedAttributes only covers description/label/message, so these behavioral inputs cannot be configured declaratively.

The dragging box-shadow uses a hardcoded color `rgba(15, 23, 42, 0.05)` instead of a --boe-token-* shadow/elevation token, breaking the token convention and likely mismatching dark theme where a cool slate shadow is inappropriate.

The `description`/`message` getters are mutually self-referential fallbacks (message falls back to description, description falls back to message), which is redundant and makes the intended precedence between the two attributes unclear; the docs only use `message` so `description` is effectively dead API surface.

No disconnectedCallback and listeners are re-bound on every render; while full innerHTML replacement discards the old nodes so listeners don't strictly leak, there is no explicit cleanup and the pattern couples state changes to full listener re-creation on every drag event.

summary: Visually polished with correct token usage and a proper brand focus ring, but the interaction model is incomplete and the public API is mismatched with its own documented example — the docs pass options/{label,value} while the component only understands items/{id,label}, so the shipped example renders an empty, non-functional dropdown.

issues.description: Docs/API contract mismatch. docs-site/examples.ts sets element.options = [{label,value}] and value:"modified", but the component exposes no `options` property/attribute (only `items`) and its items use `{id,label}` not `{value,label}`. The `set` helper just assigns a dead `options` property, so no items ever render and value "modified" matches nothing — the documented example is broken. Sibling components (combobox, select) use options/value; dropdown should follow the same convention.

Wrong ARIA menu semantics for a value picker. Trigger declares aria-haspopup="menu" but the container has no role="menu" and items are plain <button> with no role="menuitem" (or better, role="listbox"/role="option" for selection). Selection is tracked via data-selected="true" instead of aria-selected, so screen readers get no selected state. There is no aria-controls/aria-activedescendant linking the trigger to the menu/active item.

No focus management or keyboard navigation inside the menu. Opening does not move focus to an item; ArrowDown/ArrowUp/Home/End/typeahead do nothing between items (trigger keydown only opens the menu). After the innerHTML re-render on open, focus is lost entirely, so keyboard users open the menu and land nowhere.

No dismissal affordances. There is no document/outside-click listener and no Escape key handler, so once open the menu can only be closed by re-clicking the trigger or selecting an item. Standard popup dismissal behavior is missing.

Full innerHTML re-render on every open/close/select (render() rewrites shadowRoot.innerHTML and re-attaches all listeners each time). This destroys DOM state, wipes focus, and is brittle; incremental toggling of the menu / open attribute would be far safer.

Missing states. No active/pressed style on trigger or items, no error state, no loading state, and no empty-state handling — when items is empty an opened menu renders as an empty padded box. Per-item disabled is also unsupported (only the whole trigger can be disabled).

Not form-associated. As a forms/ component it uses no ElementInternals and emits no hidden input, so its value will not participate in native form submission. It also uses a custom "value-changed" event rather than the conventional "change"/"input" events.

Redundant double render on value set. The `value` setter writes valueInternal, calls setAttribute("value", ...) which fires attributeChangedCallback (re-setting valueInternal and calling render()), then the setter calls render() again — two renders per programmatic value change.

Spacing and radii use ad hoc rem magic numbers (padding 0.6rem/2.35rem, radius 0.7rem/0.75rem/0.5rem) rather than Blueprint spacing/radius tokens, so rhythm isn't guaranteed to track the design system. Selected items are indicated only by background+brand text color with no check indicator.

summary: A functional dual-listbox that leans heavily on --boe-token-* variables via color-mix and reflects its attributes correctly, but it over-styles with non-Blueprint gradients/shadows and hardcoded slate rgba values, and it has real accessibility gaps: focus is destroyed on every re-render and there is no listbox keyboard navigation.

issues.description: render() replaces the entire shadowRoot.innerHTML on every interaction (toggleSelection, moveSelected via emitValueChanged/attributeChangedCallback). After clicking or moving an item the focused option/button is destroyed and focus falls back to document.body, so keyboard users lose their place entirely. No focus is restored post-render (lines 190-419).

No listbox keyboard interaction: the ARIA listbox pattern requires arrow-key navigation with roving tabindex, but each option is a separate <button> tab stop and there is no ArrowUp/Down/Home/End handling. Options are only toggled via click/Enter/Space on individual buttons (lines 401-411).
ARIA structure is invalid: options are

Heavy multi-stop linear-gradients, inset highlights and drop shadows on panels, options and move buttons depart from Box Blueprint's flat, clean aesthetic — the design is over-decorated rather than production-authentic (lines 240-249, 273-303, 336-361).

Multiple hardcoded non-token colors bypass the token system: box-shadow uses rgba(255,255,255,0.8) and rgba(15,23,42,0.04) (a purple slate, not Box cool-neutral), aria-selected inset uses rgba(0,97,213,0.06), and the empty state border uses rgba(214,224,234,0.72) (lines 247-248, 303, 368).

Radii and spacing use arbitrary rem values off any 4px/Blueprint grid — 1.05rem, 0.95rem, 0.9rem panel/option/button radii and 2.9rem square move buttons — instead of consistent design-system radius tokens/steps (lines 239, 272, 332-333, 369).

No active/pressed state on options or the move buttons — only hover, focus-visible, disabled and aria-selected are styled. Box Blueprint controls provide a distinct pressed treatment (SurfaceSurfaceBrandPressed exists in tokens) (lines 269-362).

Transient highlight selections (availableSelection/chosenSelection sets) are never cleared when options or value change via attribute. Externally updating options can leave stale values selected in the sets, which then act on a mismatched list on the next move (lines 22-23, 83-99).

Redundant double-render: the value setter calls render() and also setAttribute("value", …), which triggers attributeChangedCallback that re-parses and renders again; similarly emitValueChanged sets the attribute and relies on the attribute callback to render. The render path is triggered twice per value change (lines 73-77, 101-111, 83-99).

Only a non-standard "value-changed" CustomEvent is dispatched; there is no conventional "change" event, and no event fires when the transient selection highlight changes, limiting host integration (lines 101-111).

The `title` getter/setter overrides HTMLElement's native `title` property and reflects to the native `title` attribute. In the docs example `<box-empty-state title="No results">`, this renders a native browser tooltip ('No results') on hover over the whole host element, an unintended side effect. A non-colliding attribute like `heading` (as sibling components use, e.g. error-mask/nudge) should be used instead.

The action button hover state hardcodes `color-mix(... black 12%)` instead of consuming the existing `--boe-token-surface-surface-brand-hover` (#0057c0) token, and there is no active/pressed style despite `--boe-token-surface-surface-brand-pressed` (#004eaa) existing. This bypasses the Blueprint palette for interactive states.

The primary action button implements only hover and focus-visible. No :active/pressed feedback and no disabled state are provided, so a consumer cannot present a disabled or in-progress action. The focus-visible ring is a low-opacity (34% transparent) brand outline rather than a solid brand focus ring, making it faint against the light secondary surface.

The title is rendered as a plain `<strong part="title">` with no heading semantics (no role="heading"/aria-level and no `<h*>`), so assistive tech gets no heading structure for the empty state. There is also no aria-labelledby tying the region to its title.

The container uses role="status" aria-live="polite". Because render() runs on connectedCallback and on every observedAttribute change (re-writing innerHTML each time), the empty state is announced on mount and re-announced on any attribute update — noisy for what is typically static content. A live region is usually unnecessary for a persistent empty state.

The `message`/`description` attributes are a redundant, mutually-referential pair (message getter falls back to description and vice-versa), both listed in observedAttributes. This duplicated surface is confusing with no clear precedence documented and adds no real capability.

render() replaces the entire shadowRoot.innerHTML on every attribute change and re-attaches the click listener each time. Beyond being inefficient, any focus on the action button is lost whenever an attribute updates. There is also no illustration/icon slot or secondary-action support, which are standard for a production empty-state pattern.

Spacing, radius and padding use ad-hoc rem values (0.55rem gap, 0.95rem radius, 0.58rem/1.05rem button padding, 2rem/1.5rem padding) rather than a shared Blueprint spacing/radius scale, so rhythm is not guaranteed to match sibling components.

summary: A clean, functional empty-state that correctly uses --boe-token-* variables with fallbacks and dispatches a composed action event, but it is middling on fidelity and robustness: it overrides the native `title` (causing a stray browser tooltip), skips existing brand hover/pressed tokens, lacks heading semantics, and offers no active/disabled states or illustration/secondary-action slots.

A tidy, well-escaped error mask with sensible attribute reflection and a retry event, but its entire danger color scheme references non-existent token names and never themes, and it reflects a heading into the global `title` attribute. Solid bones, real fidelity and theming gaps.

issues.description: The danger palette uses fabricated token names that don't exist in the registry: `--boe-token-stroke-stroke-danger`, `--boe-token-surface-surface-danger`, and `--boe-token-text-text-danger` (lines 99, 101, 111-113). The registry defines `SurfaceStatusSurfaceError` (→ `--boe-token-surface-status-surface-error`) and has no danger/stroke/text-danger keys at all. Every error-colored element (border, panel bg, icon bg, icon color) therefore always resolves to the hardcoded hex fallback (#f0b7b2/#fdecea/#b3261e) and never picks up the themed error red (#ed3757 light / #ff5a75 dark).

Backgrounds are built with `color-mix(... , white 40%)` and `white 45%` (lines 101, 111). Mixing toward literal white always lightens, so in the dark theme (box-dark) these panels render as a washed-out light-pink block on a dark surface instead of a dark-tinted error surface. The mix should be against a surface token, not `white`.

`title` is registered in observedAttributes and reflected via the setter (lines 19, 35-41). `title` is a global HTML attribute that produces a native browser tooltip on hover, so setting the heading via `title` makes the whole element show a redundant OS tooltip and collides with standard DOM semantics. The heading should live solely under `heading` (or a namespaced attribute).

Every attributeChangedCallback rebuilds the whole shadow DOM via `this.shadowRoot.innerHTML = ...` (line 86). If a keyboard/AT user has focused the retry button when any attribute changes, the button node is destroyed and recreated, silently dropping focus. It also re-creates the `role=alert` node, causing screen readers to re-announce on any content update.

Spacing, radius and sizing are hand-tuned arbitrary values off the Blueprint 4/8px rhythm: gap 0.55rem, radius 0.95rem, margin-top 0.4rem, padding 0.58rem 1.05rem (lines 97, 96, 140-141). No spacing/radius tokens are used, so the component won't track the design system's scale.

The heading is rendered as a `<strong>` (line 163) rather than a real heading element and the section is not tied to it via aria-labelledby. Inside role=alert everything is read on mount, but the title carries no heading semantics for document structure/navigation.
The retry button implements :hover and :focus-visible but has no :active/pressed state (lines 146-153) and no disabled affordance, so it lacks the press feedback expected of a Box primary button.
`role="alert"` plus `aria-live="assertive"` on the same node (line 155) is redundant — the alert role already implies an assertive live region. Harmless but non-idiomatic.

The focus-visible ring is a soft `color-mix(surface-brand 34%, transparent)` outline (lines 150-153) rather than the crisp solid brand focus ring; it reads faint against the light-pink panel and doesn't match a standard 2px brand focus indicator.

summary: A decent baseline dropdown-menu adapter with real token usage, ::part styling, and sensible ARIA scaffolding, but it lacks the core WAI-ARIA menu keyboard navigation, has no attribute API/reflection, and ships a docs example that renders nothing because it relies on a non-existent `actions` property.

issues.description: The docs-site example (examples.ts ~line 424) sets `actions: [...]` as a property, but BoxExplorerActionMenuElement has no `actions` setter, and never receives a `controller` or `itemId`. render() derives actions only from `controllerValue.getItemActions(itemId)`, so with itemIdValue null it takes the early-return branch and emits `` — the example renders nothing visible. The documented API and the real API do not match.

Menu items use role="menuitem" inside role="menu" but there is no roving keyboard navigation: only Escape is handled on menu items (lines 282-292). ArrowUp/ArrowDown/Home/End to move focus between items — required by the WAI-ARIA menu pattern — are not implemented, so a keyboard user can open the menu but cannot traverse it with arrows.

No `static get observedAttributes` and no `attributeChangedCallback`; `itemId`/`controller` are settable only via JS properties. The element implements none of the design-system attribute-reflection convention (no attribute<->property sync, no reflected `open` state on the host), so it cannot be configured or styled declaratively from markup.

Action items support no disabled, selected, or loading state. Menu items are always plain enabled buttons — there is no `aria-disabled`/`disabled` reflection from the action model, no selected styling, and `invokeItemAction` (potentially async) is fired without awaiting, so there is no loading or error affordance; the menu just closes immediately regardless of outcome (lines 262-281).

The menu only dismisses on Escape or item activation. There is no outside-click / focusout dismissal, so clicking elsewhere on the page leaves the popup open — a departure from standard menu behavior.

render() rewrites `shadowRoot.innerHTML` wholesale on every open/close, selection change, and items change (lines 117-243). This re-parses the entire <style> block, tears down and recreates all DOM, and re-attaches every event listener each time — inefficient and brittle, and it relies on queueMicrotask hacks (lines 288-299) to restore focus that the full re-render destroys.

While base colors reference --boe-token-* vars, the elevation and tint values are hardcoded and untokenized: box-shadows like rgba(15,23,42,0.04), rgba(0,67,146,0.12), rgba(0,97,213,0.18), the inset rgba(255,255,255,0.82) highlight, and the #eef4fb tint (lines 141,146-148,171,205). Radii are also inconsistent and off-scale (0.75rem trigger, 0.95rem menu, 0.7rem item), rather than a single Blueprint radius token.

After an action is invoked by click, the menu closes and render() runs but focus is not returned to the trigger (only the Escape-on-item path at lines 284-291 restores it). Mouse/keyboard activation of an item drops focus to <body>.

The element subscribes to `selectionChanged` and calls render() on every selection change (lines 81-85), but selection state never influences this menu's output — every selection change triggers a full wasteful re-render.

The trigger glyph is a raw Unicode ellipsis character `⋯` (line 240) instead of using the design system's iconography (box-iconography), so the overflow affordance is inconsistent with other components' icon rendering.

summary: A clean, controller-driven breadcrumbs adapter with correct token usage and a Box-consistent focus ring, but it has notable accessibility gaps (no list semantics, no aria-current/aria-label, unhidden separators, focus loss on re-render) and no attribute API/reflection.

issues.description:
The breadcrumb trail has no accessible list semantics or labeling. The
has no aria-label (e.g. "Breadcrumb"), and crumbs are bare

The current/last folder gets no aria-current="page". It is styled distinctly (last-of-type bold/dark, lines 139-142) but remains a plain interactive button with no ARIA state, so assistive tech cannot tell the user which crumb represents the current location.

No observedAttributes, attributeChangedCallback, or attribute reflection. The element exposes zero configurable attributes and does not follow the design system's attribute-reflection convention; it is entirely property/controller-driven, so it cannot be configured or styled declaratively from markup.

Re-render wipes and rebuilds shadowRoot.innerHTML on every breadcrumbsChanged/folderLoaded/folderChanged event (lines 90-155). If a user has keyboard focus on a breadcrumb button when any of these fire, the DOM is destroyed and focus is lost to the document body — a focus-management bug during navigation.
The "/" separators (line 87, /) are not aria-hidden, so screen readers announce "slash" between every crumb. Separators should be decorative (aria-hidden="true").

Clicking a breadcrumb calls controller.navigateTo() directly (lines 158-163) but dispatches no navigation/selection event (e.g. breadcrumb-selected). Consumers cannot intercept or observe a crumb click; only the re-emitted controller events (breadcrumbs-changed, folder-loaded, folder-changed) are surfaced.

Missing several relevant states: no :active press feedback, no disabled treatment, and no loading indication while a folder navigation is in flight (folderLoaded triggers a re-render but the trail shows no pending/loading affordance). The current crumb is also still a live button rather than a non-interactive current-page element.
The current (last) crumb remains clickable and its handler calls navigateTo() with the folder the user is already in — a redundant/no-op navigation that can trigger an unnecessary reload.

Re-renders on all three subscribed events (breadcrumbsChanged, folderLoaded, folderChanged) even when the breadcrumb array is unchanged, and each render tears down and rebuilds the full innerHTML including the <style> block and re-binds click listeners — inefficient and unnecessary churn.

Spacing and typography use arbitrary hardcoded rem values (padding 0.3rem/0.55rem, gap 0.15rem, font-size 0.92rem/0.85rem/0.9rem, lines 102-153) instead of Blueprint spacing/size tokens. Color tokens are used correctly, but the rhythm is not token-driven.

summary: A thin controller-driven listbox with solid color-token usage and a reasonable keyboard/ARIA foundation, but it is a zero-value empty alias of explorer-list with no attribute API, several ARIA correctness bugs, and missing error/disabled/active states.

issues.description: BoxExplorerItemsElement is a completely empty subclass (`export class BoxExplorerItemsElement extends BoxExplorerListElement {}`) — <box-explorer-items> is byte-for-byte identical to <box-explorer-list> with no added markup, styling, or behavior. It ships as a duplicate catalog entry with no differentiation, which is confusing and adds maintenance surface for no benefit.

No public attribute API at all: there is no static `observedAttributes`, no `attributeChangedCallback`, and no attribute-to-property reflection. The element is driven exclusively by an imperative JS `controller` property, so it cannot be configured or hydrated from HTML markup (e.g. `<box-explorer-items selectable multi-select>`), breaking the design system's reflection convention used by other components.

Error state is unhandled despite being available. ExplorerState exposes `error: ExplorerErrorState | null` (types.ts line 85), but render() never reads it and bindController() never subscribes to any error event (only itemsChanged/itemActivated/itemActionInvoked/loadingChanged/paginationChanged/selectionChanged). A failed load therefore renders as a silent empty/stale list with no error message and no re-render.

No active/pressed or disabled states. Items and buttons define :hover and :focus-visible but no `:active` pressed feedback, and there is no disabled styling or `disabled`/`aria-disabled` handling for items or the load-more button, so unavailable actions cannot be visually or programmatically communicated.

Multi-select listbox is mislabeled. Selection is a toggle (`toggleSelection`) allowing several items to carry `aria-selected="true"` simultaneously, but the `<ul role="listbox">` never sets `aria-multiselectable="true"`. Screen readers announce a single-select listbox, misrepresenting the interaction model.

Interactive controls are nested inside `role="option"` context. Each row renders per-item action `<button>`s inside the same `<li>` as the option, and the option itself is a `<button role="option">`. Placing focusable/interactive elements within (or beside) an option is an ARIA listbox anti-pattern — assistive tech does not reliably expose nested interactive content within options.

The empty-state node `
  • No items loaded
    • ` is a direct child of `role="listbox"` with no `role="option"` or `role="presentation"`, producing an invalid listbox child. It is also not announced as a status/empty region.

render() reassigns `shadowRoot.innerHTML` in full on every controller event AND on every arrow-key navigation, destroying and recreating all DOM plus re-attaching every click/keydown listener each time, then restoring focus manually via queueMicrotask. This is inefficient and brittle (focus flicker, lost transient state, listener churn) versus targeted DOM updates or a keyed diff.

A single click both toggles selection (`toggleSelection`) and activates/navigates (`activateItem`) the item. There is no way to select an item without also triggering navigation, coupling two distinct intents and making multi-select via click impractical.

Keyboard support covers ArrowUp/Down/Home/End/Enter/Space with roving tabindex, but there is no type-ahead (first-letter) navigation expected of a listbox, and the load-more and per-item action buttons sit outside the roving tabindex model with default tabindex, giving an inconsistent focus order within the widget.

Spacing, radius, and font sizes are ad hoc magic numbers (radii 0.95rem/0.7rem/0.6rem, paddings like 0.15rem 0.35rem, font-sizes 0.94rem/0.82rem/0.88rem) rather than Blueprint spacing/radius tokens, and the shell shadow uses a hardcoded `rgba(15,23,42,0.04)` instead of a token. Color tokens are used well, but the spatial rhythm is not tokenized.

Stale focusItemId after navigation creates a keyboard trap. The item click handler sets this.focusItemId = itemId, then activateItem() navigates into a folder and loads a brand-new item set that does not contain that id. On the next render, focusTarget = (this.focusItemId ?? first item) resolves to the now-missing folder id, so EVERY item gets tabindex="-1" and the listbox has no tab stop. The post-render queueMicrotask focus() also finds no match, so focus is dropped to <body>. focusItemId is never reset on navigation/reload (adapters/list.ts lines ~99, ~121, ~305-312, ~360-366).

Error state is never rendered. The controller exposes state.error (set by applyLoadFailure), and the sibling box-content-explorer renders a [part="error"] block, but this adapter's render() only reads state.items/pagination/loading. A failed load silently shows the empty 'No items loaded' placeholder with no message, part, or ARIA alert — the failure is invisible to the user.

No attribute API at all: the class defines no static observedAttributes, no attributeChangedCallback, and no attribute reflection. The component is configurable only via the imperative JS `controller` property. This violates the design system's observedAttributes/attribute-reflection convention that box-content-explorer follows (observedAttributes for language/page-size/root-folder-id/selection-mode/token).

role="listbox" is used but aria-multiselectable is never set, even though the controller's default selectionMode is 'multiple' (controller.ts get selectionMode() returns 'multiple') and multiple items can be selected. Assistive tech announces it as single-select, contradicting actual behavior.

Clicking or pressing Enter/Space on an item both toggles selection AND activates it (toggleSelection + activateItem in the same handler, lines ~305-313/329-331). In a listbox, focus movement should not mutate selection and activation should be distinct; conflating them means every focus-driven click reselects and, for folders, immediately navigates away, which is confusing for keyboard and screen-reader users.

No live region for loading or status. Only aria-busy="true" toggles on the shell (which merely dims opacity to 0.72); there is no aria-live announcement when items load, the list becomes busy, or an error occurs, so screen-reader users get no feedback on async state changes.

Focus-stealing across shared controllers. render() runs on every subscribed event (itemsChanged, loadingChanged, selectionChanged, paginationChanged) and, whenever this.focusItemId is set, unconditionally re-focuses that item via queueMicrotask. Because the docs example drives multiple adapters from one shared ContentExplorerController, a selection or load event originating in a sibling adapter will yank focus back into this list.

Missing :active/pressed and :disabled states. No :active rule exists for [part~="item"], [part~="item-action"], or [part="load-more"] despite the SurfaceSurfaceBrandPressed token (#004eaa) being available; and item-action / load-more buttons are never rendered with a disabled state or styled :disabled, so they cannot reflect an unavailable/in-flight action.

Hardcoded, non-tokenized values: box-shadow: 0 1px 2px rgba(15, 23, 42, 0.04) is a raw slate rgba rather than a --boe-token-* elevation token, and spacing/radius/font-size values are arbitrary and unsystematic (radius 0.95/0.7/0.6rem mixed; font-size 0.94/0.82/0.88rem; gaps 0.15/0.3/0.5rem) rather than aligned to a Blueprint spacing scale.

Dead CSS: an input[type="checkbox"] { accent-color: ... } rule is included (line ~255) but the template never renders any checkbox — leftover styling that inflates the stylesheet and signals an unfinished multi-select affordance.

summary: box-explorer-list is a controller-driven adapter with clean token usage and roving-tabindex keyboard nav, but it has a real keyboard-trap bug (stale focusItemId leaves no tab stop after navigating into a folder), silently swallows error state, exposes no attribute API/reflection, and omits active/disabled/multiselect handling — solid scaffolding, not production quality.

A competent controller-driven table adapter with solid token usage, real focus-visible rings, and safe HTML escaping, but it exposes no attribute API/observedAttributes, re-renders the entire table via innerHTML on every event (losing focus), and lacks grid keyboard semantics.

issues.description: The element defines no `static get observedAttributes()`, no `attributeChangedCallback`, and no attribute reflection. It is purely property-driven via the `controller` setter, so the documented `<box-explorer-table></box-explorer-table>` usage has zero declarative/attribute API — violating the design system's attribute-reflection convention (contrast content-explorer.ts:75 which does implement observedAttributes).

render() rewrites the entire `shadowRoot.innerHTML` on every controller event (itemsChanged, selectionChanged, loadingChanged, etc. — table.ts:70). This discards and rebuilds all rows/buttons on each selection toggle, destroying keyboard focus, checkbox focus, and scroll position, and re-attaching every listener each time — an inefficient and accessibility-harmful re-render strategy.

Rows are a static HTML `<table>` with `aria-selected` on each `<tr>` (table.ts:98). `aria-selected` is only valid/announced when the container carries grid/treegrid/listbox roles; on a plain table it is not a recognized selection state. Either add `role="grid"` with proper row/gridcell roles or use a different selected indication.

No grid keyboard model: rows are not focusable and there is no arrow-key navigation or roving tabindex. Navigation relies solely on sequential Tab through every checkbox, name button, and action menu, which is tedious for large lists and does not match a Box content table's expected keyboard interaction.

Clicking the item name button both toggles selection AND activates the item (table.ts:319-320: toggleSelection then activateItem). Opening a file also silently mutates selection state, conflicting with the dedicated selection checkbox and producing surprising UX — activation and selection should be distinct.

Missing several applicable states: no `:active` press state on the name button or load-more, no disabled state, and no error state. The header 'Select' column has no select-all checkbox despite multi-selection being supported, so there is no bulk-select affordance or indeterminate state.

Loading is only communicated via `aria-busy` + `opacity: 0.72` on the shell; there is no skeleton, spinner, or row placeholder. The Load-more button is simply removed while `state.loading` (table.ts:124), so a user who activated it loses focus (focus falls to body) with no in-place busy/disabled feedback.

Spacing and radii use arbitrary hardcoded values rather than a Blueprint token scale: border-radius 0.95rem / 0.6rem / 0.7rem and padding like 0.45rem/0.6rem/0.8rem are off-scale magic numbers, and the shell box-shadow `rgba(15,23,42,0.04)` is a hardcoded color instead of an elevation/shadow token.

Extensive reliance on hardcoded hex fallbacks inside color-mix (e.g. #e8e8e8, #0061d5, #f4f4f4) means that if the --boe-token-* variables are absent the component silently renders a full ad-hoc palette rather than failing loudly, masking missing-token integration during theming.

No observedAttributes, attributeChangedCallback, or attribute reflection anywhere. The component is configurable only via the JS `controller` property; it cannot be driven or reflected via HTML attributes, violating the design system's reflection convention that peer components follow. There is no static observedAttributes getter at all.

The toolbar container (<div part="toolbar">) has no role="toolbar" and no aria-label. Blueprint/ARIA toolbar semantics and arrow-key roving focus are entirely absent; it is just a bare div wrapping two buttons.

Error state is never rendered. render() subscribes to loadFailed and the controller exposes state.error with a message (controller.ts applyLoadFailure), but the template only ever prints "loading"/"ready" — after a failed load the status still shows "ready" and the error message is silently dropped.

render() replaces shadowRoot.innerHTML on every controller event (loadingChanged, selectionChanged, loadFailed). This destroys and rebuilds all DOM nodes, so if the user has keyboard focus on the Refresh/Clear button when any event fires, focus is lost and reset to the body — a real focus-management regression. It also re-parses CSS and re-attaches listeners on every state change unnecessarily.

The status span ("loading"/"ready") and selection-count span ("N selected") are not live regions (no aria-live / role=status) and the toolbar sets no aria-busy while loading. Screen-reader users get no announcement when loading state or selection count changes.

No active/pressed state on either button. The token set provides --boe-token-surface-surface-brand-pressed and the buttons animate box-shadow/background, but there is no :active rule, so presses give no visual feedback.

Hardcoded, non-tokenized cool-slate drop shadow `box-shadow: 0 1px 2px rgba(15,23,42,0.04)` clashes with the design system's warm-neutral palette and bypasses tokens entirely. There is no shadow/elevation token used.

Border radii of 0.9rem (toolbar) and 0.7rem (buttons) are considerably rounder than Box Blueprint's tighter control radii (~4-6px), and status text is rendered lowercase ("loading"/"ready") which reads unpolished versus proper sentence/label casing.

Dispatched event names are mechanically derived from internal controller event keys via toKebabCase (loading-changed, selection-changed, load-failed) with no component namespace prefix, so they are prone to collision and are coupled to internal naming rather than a deliberate public event contract.

summary: A functional but thin adapter: it uses --boe-token-* variables correctly and covers hover/focus-visible/disabled/loading, but it ships no attribute API or reflection, lacks toolbar role/aria-live semantics, never surfaces the error state it subscribes to, and rebuilds innerHTML on every event which drops keyboard focus. Solidly below Box production quality.

issues.description: render() rewrites this.shadowRoot.innerHTML on every attributeChangedCallback (including toggling disabled). This destroys and recreates the <slot>, causing slotted controls to be re-distributed, losing focus and any transient state on each label/description/disabled change. State-driven updates should mutate existing nodes, not blow away the whole shadow tree.

syncDisabledControls() querySelector includes '[disabled], [aria-disabled]' in the selector. This matches controls the consumer already disabled; when the fieldset is disabled it skips setting the data-marker on them (guarded by !control.hasAttribute('disabled')), which is correct, but the aria-disabled branch means an already-aria-disabled but not attribute-disabled control gets a real 'disabled' attribute added and marked — so re-enabling the fieldset silently strips the control's original aria-disabled intent boundary. The dual selector is brittle and conflates 'controls to manage' with 'controls already off'.

When the 'label' attribute is empty, no <legend> is rendered and the <fieldset> has no accessible name, so the group is announced as an unnamed grouping. A fieldset's whole purpose is a named group; there is no aria-label/aria-labelledby fallback.

The description

is placed inside the

and referenced via aria-describedby on the fieldset, but individual slotted controls are not associated with the description. Screen-reader users focusing a child input will not hear the group description; there is no propagation of aria-describedby to controls.

Only one design token is used (--boe-token-text-text-secondary for legend/description). Spacing (0.35rem, 0.85rem, 0.75rem), font sizes (0.8rem, 0.86rem), letter-spacing (0.06em) and the disabled opacity (0.6) are all hardcoded magic numbers rather than Blueprint spacing/typography/radius tokens. No border, surface, or radius tokens are used at all.

No error state (no invalid/error attribute, no error message styling or aria-invalid propagation) and no focus-visible brand ring anywhere. For a form grouping component, an error/validation-summary state is a core expectation and is entirely absent.
The component dispatches no events. There is no change/input aggregation or any lifecycle event, so consumers cannot observe group-level state (e.g., disabled toggled) except by reading attributes.

The <fieldset> carries both a native 'disabled' attribute and a redundant data-disabled="true"/"false" attribute used only to apply opacity:0.6. The native :disabled pseudo-class could style it; the parallel data attribute is duplicative state that can drift.

observedAttributes/reflection covers label, description, disabled but there is no 'name' or grouping identifier and no reflected error/required attributes, limiting the component's usefulness as a real form primitive.

summary: A minimal fieldset wrapper that is functional but under-built: it re-renders its entire shadow DOM on every attribute change (destroying the slot and focus), leans on hardcoded magic numbers with only a single design token, and lacks error/focus-visible states, description-to-control association, and any events. The disabled-mirroring logic works but uses a brittle selector.

A functional composite pattern with solid attribute/reflection/event API and token usage, but it lacks nearly all interaction states, has real focus-loss/re-render bugs, and undercuts fidelity with off-grid spacing, hardcoded white in color-mix (dark-theme breakage), and a misused text token as a button border.

issues.description: No interaction states are implemented at all. Buttons ([part="action"]) and setting rows have no :hover, :focus-visible, or :active styling, despite tokens existing for exactly these (--boe-token-surface-surface-hover, --boe-token-surface-surface-brand-hover, --boe-token-surface-surface-brand-pressed, --boe-token-stroke-stroke-hover). There is no brand focus ring anywhere.

Every checkbox toggle triggers a full shadow-DOM teardown: updateValue() calls setAttribute("value", ...) which fires attributeChangedCallback → render(), which reassigns shadowRoot.innerHTML and re-binds all listeners (lines 130, 84-99, 190). This destroys and recreates the checkbox the user just clicked, losing keyboard focus and thrashing the DOM on each interaction.

Toggling a checkbox re-renders the whole tree, so keyboard focus is lost after every change — the user is dumped back to the top of the component. Combined with the missing focus-visible ring, keyboard operation is broken/disorienting.

The title is a plain <div part="title"> (line 324) rather than a heading (h2/h3 or role=heading), and neither the <section part="builder"> region nor the settings/fields <section>s have an accessible name (no aria-label/aria-labelledby tying them to their [part="section-title"] divs). Screen-reader structure is flat.

The primary button sets border-color: var(--boe-token-text-text, #1f1e1b) (line 318) — using the body-text token as a border on a brand-filled button produces a near-black outline around the blue button. It also hardcodes color: white (line 320) instead of --boe-token-text-text-on-brand, which exists in the token set.

color-mix() calls hardcode white as a mix partner: 'white 6%' on the builder background (line 204), 'white 88%' on the required pill (line 290), 'white 6%'. In dark theme the surrounding surfaces flip dark but these mixes stay lightened toward white, so the required badge and card backgrounds will not re-theme correctly.

Spacing and radii are off the Blueprint 4/8px grid and not token-derived: padding 1.1rem (17.6px), 0.85rem (13.6px), 0.72rem (11.52px), gaps 0.45rem/0.35rem/0.7rem, radii 1rem/0.9rem. Rhythm is arbitrary rather than a consistent scale.

The checked/selected setting row has no visual affordance — the container keeps the same background whether checked or not (--boe-token-surface-item-surface-selected exists and is unused). Buttons also have no disabled or loading state, so async actions like 'Save request' / 'Copy upload link' cannot show progress or be locked.

Action buttons (copy-link, preview, save) and their labels are hardcoded in render() (lines 330-332) and not configurable via an attribute, unlike the sibling task-assignment-panel which accepts an 'actions' array. Also, setting the value property both calls render() directly (line 77) and triggers attributeChangedCallback → render() (line 99), causing a redundant double render/parse per property set.

attributeChangedCallback calls render() (full shadowRoot.innerHTML rewrite) on EVERY attribute change, including attributes the component sets on itself. Clicking a filter chip calls setAttribute('filters', ...) -> attributeChangedCallback -> the entire bar is destroyed and rebuilt. Same for select change (sets 'sort-value'/'view-value') and the input 'change' event (sets 'query'). This tears down and recreates all DOM/listeners, causing focus loss and flicker after every interaction, e.g. the search input loses focus on blur/Enter and a toggled chip's focus ring vanishes.

No hover, active, or disabled states are implemented for inputs, selects, or filter chips. Only :focus-visible and the selected chip state exist. Tokens like --boe-token-surface-surface-hover and --boe-token-stroke-stroke-hover are available but unused, so interactive controls feel flat and non-Box on pointer hover/press.

No disabled/error/loading affordances at all — there is no 'disabled' in observedAttributes, no disabled styling, and controls cannot be programmatically disabled. A production filter bar typically needs at least a disabled state while data is loading.

Select value/state can silently desync. When sortValue/viewValue is '' (the docs example provides sortOptions/viewOptions but no matching selected value), no <option> is marked selected, so the browser visually selects the first option while sortValueInternal/viewValueInternal remain ''. There is also no empty/placeholder option, so getState() reports '' even though a real option is displayed.

The filter chips container (<div part="filters">) has no grouping semantics or label — no role="group"/"toolbar" and no aria-label like 'Filters'. Screen-reader users get a set of unlabeled toggle buttons with no indication they form the filter group, and there is no visible 'Filters' heading for that row unlike the Search/Sort/View fields.
The <section> already carries aria-label={label}, and a separate <div part="label"> repeats the same text, producing a redundant/duplicated announcement of the label.
renderOptionList declares a partName parameter (and callers pass 'sort'/'view') that is never used in the method body — dead argument indicating incomplete part exposure for options.

Spacing, radii, and font sizes are hardcoded rem values (0.9rem, 1rem, 0.85rem, radius 1rem/0.85rem/999px, 0.82rem labels) rather than Blueprint spacing/radius scale tokens; and both the bar label and every field label are uppercase 700 with letter-spacing, which double-stacks heavy all-caps treatment and reads heavier than Box's typical field labels.

No dedicated event is emitted for filter-chip toggles distinct from sort/view/query changes — everything funnels into a single 'value-changed'. Consumers wanting to react specifically to a filter toggle (vs a sort change) must diff full state, and there is no per-filter detail (which value was added/removed).

summary: A reasonably polished token-driven filter bar with good attribute reflection, events, and accessible labels/aria-pressed chips, but let down by a full innerHTML re-render on every self-set attribute change (focus loss/flicker) and thin state coverage (no hover/active/disabled).

A reasonably structured panel with good --boe-token-* coverage and clean event dispatch, but it undershoots on interactive states, accessibility semantics, and has a real native-property collision on `title`. Solid skeleton, not production-grade.

issues.description: The class overrides the native HTMLElement.title property (lines 84-90) with governance-specific semantics and a hardcoded 'Governance' default. This collides with the built-in tooltip attribute: any consumer or tooling that sets element.title to provide a browser tooltip will instead mutate the panel heading, and reading .title returns 'Governance' instead of the DOM norm of ''. Rename to a non-reserved attribute like 'heading' / 'panel-title'.

No semantic heading or landmark labeling. The title is a plain <div part="title"> (lines 227-231, 363) rather than a heading element, so screen-reader heading navigation and document outline get nothing, and the <section part="panel"> has no aria-labelledby/aria-label tying it to the title. Emit an actual heading (or role=heading aria-level) and reference it via aria-labelledby.

The status badge (lines 139, 254-257) is a decorative <span> with no role="status" and no aria-live region. Because status is an observed attribute that re-renders the whole tree, dynamic compliance changes ('Compliant' -> 'Non-compliant') are never announced to assistive tech. Wrap status in a role="status"/aria-live="polite" container.

Interactive states are largely missing. Only policy has a :hover rule (309-312) and there is no :hover on [part="action"], no :active/pressed state anywhere (despite tokens SurfaceSurfaceBrandHover/Pressed existing), and no disabled, loading, error, or selected styling for any control. Actions and policies feel static/unpolished under interaction.

Hardcoded brand colors bypass the token layer: focus outline uses rgba(0,97,213,0.28) and policy hover border uses rgba(0,97,213,0.22) (lines 310, 316) instead of --boe-token-surface-surface-brand, and the primary action text uses literal `color: white` (line 357) instead of --boe-token-text-text-on-brand. The focus ring is also a low-opacity 2px outline rather than a solid brand focus ring.

The policy hover applies transform: translateY(-1px) (line 311) with no transition declared anywhere in the stylesheet, producing an instantaneous jump on hover and immediate snap on focus outlines. Add transitions for hover/focus/active to match Blueprint motion polish.

Neutral-tone chips fall through to brand styling. The base rule for [part="status"], [part="signal"], [part="policy-value"] (lines 238-252) paints a brand-blue pill, and only 'warning'/'accent' tones (and status) override it — there is no [data-tone="neutral"] rule. So a default policy value like 'Internal' / '7 years' and a neutral signal render as loud uppercase brand-blue badges, almost certainly unintended.

render() rebuilds shadowRoot.innerHTML wholesale on every attributeChangedCallback (line 198) and re-binds listeners each time. Any attribute change while a policy/action button is focused (e.g. a status update) destroys and recreates the node, silently dropping keyboard focus. Consider diffing or preserving focus across re-renders.

Policy buttons expose an accessible name assembled only from concatenated child text; the value badge is styled uppercase with letter-spacing (text-transform, lines 250-251) but carries no explicit aria-label, and clickable policies give no indication of their purpose to AT beyond raw label/value. Add an aria-label summarizing 'label: value, description'.

The 'action' event detail only carries { action: actionId } (lines 113-121), discarding the action's label/tone, while 'policy-selected' sends the full object. Inconsistent detail payloads force consumers to re-look-up action metadata they already provided.

Events are named 'action' and 'policy-selected' with no design-system prefix (e.g. 'boe-'), risking collisions with generic DOM/event names and diverging from a namespaced-event convention; 'action' in particular is a very generic bubbling+composed event name.

The selected-tile and thumbnail backgrounds hardcode `white` inside color-mix: `color-mix(in srgb, var(--boe-token-surface-surface-brand,#0061d5) 10%, white 90%)` (line 208) and `...surface-secondary 82%, white 18%)` (line 222). In the box-dark theme this produces a near-white tile/thumb on a dark surface — the mix should be against a surface token (e.g. --boe-token-surface-surface), not literal white, so it breaks dark-mode fidelity.

A dedicated selected-item token exists (SurfaceItemSurfaceSelected = #f2f7fd -> --boe-token-surface-item-surface-selected) but the selected tile instead invents its own brand color-mix (line 208). It should consume the design-system's item-selected surface token for consistency with other collection components.

Radii and spacing use off-grid values not aligned to the Blueprint scale: border-radius 0.85rem (13.6px) and 0.6rem (9.6px) (lines 195, 221), padding `1rem 0.85rem` (line 190). Blueprint uses a 4px rhythm (radius 4/6/8px); these arbitrary values read as unrhythmic.

No :active/pressed state and no disabled state. The design system ships SurfaceSurfaceBrandPressed but the tiles have no active styling, and there is no `disabled` attribute or per-item disabled handling (a disabled option can still be focused via roving tabindex, selected, and receives hover styling).

No loading state. A grid/gallery view of files is inherently async, but the component only distinguishes populated vs. empty ('No items loaded', line 250). There is no skeleton/aria-busy loading affordance.

The layout is a 2D CSS grid (auto-fill columns, line 181) but arrow-key navigation is purely linear: ArrowUp/ArrowLeft both do delta -1 and ArrowDown/ArrowRight both do +1 (lines 264-273). ArrowDown/ArrowUp should move by a row in a visual grid, or the container should use role=grid; the listbox+linear-nav mismatch is confusing with a keyboard/screen reader.

Focus can be stolen unexpectedly. render() unconditionally re-focuses the tile matching focusValue (lines 293-298), and focusValue persists after selection. Any later attributeChangedCallback-driven render (e.g. an external `items`/`value` update) will yank focus back onto the previously focused tile even if focus had moved elsewhere on the page.

The empty state renders a plain <div part="empty"> with the listbox role and its aria-label removed (lines 249-251). The accessible grouping/label is lost when there are zero items, and there is no role=status to announce emptiness.

Double render on every selection/value set. `set value` (lines 69-71) and selectValue (lines 92-94) both call setAttribute('value',…) — which synchronously triggers attributeChangedCallback -> render() — and then call render() again explicitly. Each selection rebuilds the entire shadow DOM innerHTML twice and re-attaches all click/keydown listeners twice.

Full re-render per keystroke. moveFocus/moveFocusTo replace the whole grid innerHTML and re-attach a click+keydown listener pair on every tile for each arrow press (render, lines 254-291). For large collections, roving focus becomes O(n) DOM teardown/rebuild per keypress; roving tabindex + .focus() could be updated in place instead.

The `items` setter serializes the full array to a JSON `items` attribute (line 53), which both dumps all data into the light DOM and triggers a redundant attributeChangedCallback render. A property-backed store would avoid mirroring potentially large datasets into an observed attribute.

summary: A competent, token-aware listbox grid with solid keyboard support and clean API/event surface, but it falls short of production quality: hardcoded `white` in color-mix breaks dark mode, it ignores the dedicated selected-surface token, lacks active/disabled/loading states, and uses linear arrow navigation on a 2D grid with focus-stealing re-renders.

issues.description: No aria-live region anywhere. Help text with tone="error" or "warning" functions as form validation feedback, but the container is a static role="note" with no aria-live="polite"/"assertive". When the message/tone changes dynamically (e.g. validation fails), screen reader users are never notified. Compare alert.ts which correctly uses role="status" aria-live="polite". The error/warning tones need a live region (or role="alert") to be announced.

No association mechanism with a form control. The whole purpose of a help-text component is to describe an input via aria-describedby, but the element exposes no id, no describedby helper, and renders its text only inside its own shadow DOM under role="note". A consumer cannot point an <input aria-describedby> at the actual description text, so the help text is functionally disconnected from the field it describes.

Redundant/generic accessible name.
