The token set provides SurfaceSurfaceBrandHover/SurfaceSurfaceBrandPressed and StrokeStrokeHover, none of which are used, so buttons are visually inert on hover/press — below Box production polish.

No disabled state is supported for actions. PreviewHeaderAction has no `disabled` field and buttons never receive a disabled attribute or disabled styling, so unavailable actions cannot be represented.

render() replaces the entire shadowRoot.innerHTML on every attributeChangedCallback (line 156). Any focus on an action/breadcrumb button is destroyed on each attribute update, and all listeners are re-created every render. This causes focus loss and unnecessary DOM churn versus targeted/patch updates.

The status pill (lines 218-228) is always brand-blue regardless of the `status` value. Semantic status tokens exist (SurfaceStatusSurfaceSuccess/Error/Inprogress) but are unused, so a 'Shared', 'Error', or 'In progress' status all render identically as generic brand — no semantic color mapping.

Breadcrumb buttons (line 124) never mark the current/last crumb with aria-current="page", and the trailing crumb is a clickable button like the rest, so assistive tech cannot identify the current location in the breadcrumb trail.

Hardcoded color literals bypass tokens: the primary action text uses `#fff` (line 254) instead of --boe-token-text-text-on-brand, and --_obp-brand-soft mixes against literal `white` (line 168). Spacing values (0.9rem, 1.1rem, 0.42rem, 0.55rem, 1.6rem) are arbitrary rather than drawn from a consistent Blueprint spacing scale.

The dispatched event name `action` (line 95) is extremely generic and prone to collision with native/other-component events; a namespaced name (e.g. `preview-action`) would be safer. JSON-array attributes (actions/breadcrumbs) also silently fall back to [] on malformed JSON with no warning, hiding author errors.

summary: The preview-header consistently references valid --boe-token-* variables and escapes user content, but it falls well short of Box production quality: the title lacks heading semantics, it overrides the native HTMLElement.title property, the documented `eyebrow` prop is unimplemented, and hover/active/disabled and semantic status states are missing.

A clean, correctly-tokenized single-file progress bar with solid progressbar ARIA, but its full-innerHTML re-render defeats its own width transition, it has no NaN guards, and it lacks indeterminate/error states and events.

issues.description: render() rewrites shadowRoot.innerHTML on every attributeChangedCallback, replacing the [part=indicator] node each time. Because the element is destroyed and recreated, the declared `transition: width 140ms ease` (line 113) never animates — the bar jumps instantly on value changes. The intended smooth fill is dead code. Should update the existing indicator's width/style and ARIA attributes in place instead of rebuilding markup+style on each update.

No NaN guard on numeric parsing. `value` and `max` come from `Number(getAttribute(...))` (lines 33, 41). With value="abc" or max="foo", Number()→NaN propagates: `Math.max(1, NaN)`→NaN, clamp→NaN, percentage=`Math.round(NaN)`→NaN, producing `style="width:NaN%"` and `aria-valuenow="NaN"`. Parse with a fallback (e.g. treat NaN as 0 for value, 100 for max).

No indeterminate/loading state. A progress bar frequently must represent unknown/ongoing progress (total not yet known). There is no `indeterminate` attribute, no animated barber-pole/track animation, and correct ARIA for that case (omitting aria-valuenow) is not handled. Only determinate progress is supported.

No semantic status coloring. The indicator is always brand blue (var(--boe-token-surface-surface-brand), line 112). There is no error/warning/success tone — e.g. a near-full 'Storage used' bar cannot turn red via the available SurfaceStatusSurfaceError (#ed3757) / Inprogress / Success status tokens. No `tone`/`status` attribute exists.

No events are dispatched. The component emits nothing on value change or on reaching max (e.g. a 'complete' or 'change' CustomEvent), so consumers cannot react to progress milestones without polling attributes. observedAttributes is limited to label/max/value with no indeterminate/tone.

Redundant labeling: the outer wrapper is role="group" with aria-label "{label} progress" (line 116) and the inner role="progressbar" also carries aria-label="{label}" (line 124). Screen readers announce the label twice (group then progressbar). The group role/label adds little here; consider dropping the group wrapper label or associating the visible label via aria-labelledby.

Spacing and sizing use ad-hoc rem literals (gap 0.45rem, 0.65rem; track height 0.5rem; font sizes 0.8rem/0.86rem — lines 75-102) rather than Blueprint spacing/size tokens, so the component doesn't track the system's spacing rhythm and can't be re-themed via tokens for density/radius.

No prefers-reduced-motion handling. The width transition (line 113) is unconditional; users with reduced-motion preferences get animated fills with no @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) override to disable the transition.

summary: A clean, token-driven circular progress indicator with correct core ARIA, but the stroke-dashoffset transition is dead (full re-render destroys the node), it has no indeterminate/complete states, and it double-announces to screen readers. Solid but not production-polished.

issues.description: render() reassigns shadowRoot.innerHTML wholesale on every attributeChangedCallback, destroying and recreating the <circle part="indicator"> node each time. Because a brand-new node starts at its final stroke-dashoffset, the `transition: stroke-dashoffset 140ms ease` (line 105) never animates — the intended progress animation is dead code. It should diff/update the existing circle's stroke-dashoffset rather than rebuild the DOM.

Non-numeric or empty value/max/size attributes yield NaN. Number("abc") is NaN, clamp(NaN,0,max) returns NaN, and percentage = Math.round(NaN) = NaN, so the ring renders the literal text "NaN%" and aria-valuenow="NaN". size=NaN similarly poisons Math.max(48, NaN)=NaN, breaking the SVG geometry. Inputs should be coerced with a Number.isFinite fallback.
No indeterminate/loading state. Progress rings commonly need an indeterminate mode (spinning arc, aria-valuenow omitted) for unknown-duration work; there is no attribute or rendering path for it.

Redundant labeling / double announcement. The outer <div part="ring"> has role="group" aria-label="{label} progress" AND the inner <svg> has role="progressbar" with the same aria-label plus aria-valuetext. The visible <span part="value"> (e.g. "80%") and <span part="label"> are not aria-hidden, so a screen reader announces the label, the progressbar value, and then the visible text again. Drop the wrapper role="group" and mark the visible meta text aria-hidden="true".

No visual completion/error affordance. At 100% or on failure the indicator stays plain brand blue with no success/error token (surface-status-surface-success / -error exist in the token set) to signal terminal state.
No events dispatched. The component never emits a completion or change event (e.g. a 'complete' event when value reaches max), so consumers must poll attributes to react to progress.

Typography and spacing use ad-hoc literals (font-size 0.68rem, letter-spacing 0.08em, gap 0.1rem, value font-size Math.max(0.8, size/88)rem) rather than Blueprint spacing/type tokens, so it won't track the design system's rhythm if those scales change.

summary: A cleanly-tokenized, XSS-safe stepper with solid attribute/event API, but it misuses tab/tablist ARIA for a progress indicator and has re-entrant render logic where an unknown value silently mutates state. Visually competent yet missing connector lines, completed-step checkmarks, and disabled/error/loading states.

issues.description: The component applies role="tablist"/role="tab" with aria-selected to a progress indicator (lines 100-108, 214), but there are no tab panels and no aria-controls. Tab semantics imply switchable panels; screen readers announce 'tab, selected' which misrepresents a linear progress stepper. It should use a list (role="list"/listitem or an ordered list) with aria-current="step" on the active item, or an explicit progress pattern — not tabs.

Step completion status is conveyed only visually (green-tinted marker via data-state="complete", lines 181-185). Completed and upcoming steps carry no ARIA/text indication, so a screen-reader user cannot tell which steps are done vs. remaining — only the single 'current' step is announced via aria-current. Add visually-hidden status text (e.g. 'completed', 'not started') per step.

Arrow/Home/End keydown handlers (lines 237-274) don't just move focus — they immediately overwrite the committed progress value (this.valueInternal, the value attribute) and dispatch value-changed on every press. Because navigation wraps (line 243: index>=lastIndex ? 0), pressing ArrowRight on the last step silently resets the progress value back to step 1. Focus movement should be decoupled from committing the value (roving tabindex + Enter/Space to activate), or at minimum should not wrap the committed progress state.

render() mutates state and reflects an attribute as a side effect: when valueInternal matches no item, activeIndex falls back to 0 (line 83-86) and lines 88-92 rewrite valueInternal + call setAttribute('value', ...) during rendering. That setAttribute re-triggers attributeChangedCallback → render() again, causing a redundant re-entrant second render on initial mount and any unknown value. An empty/unknown value silently snaps to the first step rather than rendering a neutral 'nothing selected' state.

No disabled state exists — neither a component-level 'disabled' attribute nor per-step disabling. Every step is always a focusable, clickable button (lines 100-108, 219-235), so consumers cannot prevent jumping to a future/locked step. There is also no error or loading state and no active/pressed styling (only hover, focus-visible, and current are styled).

Completed steps still render the step number in a tinted circle (lines 110, 181-185) rather than a checkmark, and there are no connector lines joining the step markers. Box Blueprint steppers typically show a check glyph for completed steps and a vertical/horizontal connector between markers, so the component reads as a list of cards rather than a progress track.

The 'value-changed' CustomEvent (lines 228-234, 262-268) fires on programmatic-adjacent flows but there is no validation or guard on the items attribute schema, and duplicate item.value entries resolve to the first match via findIndex (line 85) with no warning. Click and keyboard paths also duplicate the identical 'commit value + dispatch event' logic instead of sharing one helper, inviting drift.

summary: A serviceable native-fieldset radio group with decent token-based styling and XSS-safe escaping, but it re-renders its entire shadow DOM on every selection (destroying keyboard focus), has no disabled/error/active visual states, and its gradients hardcode `white` so they break in the shipped dark theme.

issues.description: Every selection triggers a full innerHTML rebuild: the change handler calls setAttribute("value", ...) (line 201), which fires attributeChangedCallback -> render() (lines 75-81), replacing the whole shadow tree. This destroys the currently-focused <input>, so keyboard focus is lost the instant a user picks an option and native radio arrow-key navigation is broken. A form component's core interaction is effectively broken for keyboard users.

No visual disabled state. When disabled, the code only adds the `disabled` attribute to each <input> (line 93); the option cards keep full-opacity backgrounds, hover styles still fire, and there is no dimming or cursor:not-allowed. A disabled group looks identical to an enabled one.

Dark theme is broken. box-dark.ts is a shipped theme, but the hover and selected backgrounds mix tokens with the literal keyword `white` (e.g. lines 152-153, 162-163: `color-mix(... white 56%)`, `white 91%`). In dark mode the token resolves dark but is blended toward white, producing washed-out light patches instead of a dark surface.

References a non-existent token `--boe-token-surface-item-surface-hover` (lines 152-153). No SurfaceItemSurfaceHover exists in box-defaults.ts or box-dark.ts (only SurfaceSurfaceHover), so the hover background always resolves to the hardcoded fallback #eef4fb rather than a real token, and won't track theme changes.

Focus ring is too faint. The focus indicator is `outline: 2px solid color-mix(... brand 26%, transparent)` (line 171), i.e. a 26%-opacity brand outline, well below the standard opaque Blueprint brand focus ring and likely failing WCAG 2.4.11/1.4.11 focus-visibility contrast. It also uses :focus-within, so it appears on mouse click, not just keyboard focus.

Missing active/pressed and error states. There is no :active feedback on the option card and no error/invalid styling or messaging, despite this being a form input where validation feedback is expected.

Not form-associated. The element does not use ElementInternals (no static formAssociated / attachInternals), so despite being a forms component it does not participate in native form submission — the selected value won't be submitted with a surrounding <form>.

Non-standard event and no event on programmatic change. It dispatches a custom `value-changed` event (lines 202-208) instead of a native-like `change`/`input`, and the value setter (lines 27-31) updates state without dispatching any event, so programmatic changes are unobservable by listeners.

The value setter double-renders: set value() calls setAttribute("value", ...) then this.render() (lines 29-30), but setAttribute already triggers attributeChangedCallback -> render(), causing two full shadow-DOM rebuilds per programmatic assignment.

The radio group `name` is regenerated with Math.random() on every render (line 88). It is internally consistent within a single render so selection works, but it is unnecessary churn and means the name is unstable across renders; combined with the full re-render this is wasteful.

Non-token, non-Blueprint decorative values: option cards use an oversized 0.95rem border-radius (line 134) that reads more like a card than a Blueprint form control, and the selected-state box-shadow hardcodes cool-slate rgba(15,23,42,...) and rgba(255,255,255,...) (lines 166-167) rather than surface/shadow tokens.

Dragging tears down the active thumb mid-interaction. Every 'input' event calls syncValue(), which calls setAttribute('start'/'end') (lines 131-132). That fires attributeChangedCallback (line 107) which unconditionally calls render() (line 116), replacing shadowRoot.innerHTML (line 166) and destroying the exact <input> the user is dragging. Continuous drag breaks: the thumb loses pointer tracking and the interaction stutters on every step. syncValue should not write attributes (or render() must not rebuild DOM) during live input.

Both range inputs are wrapped in a single <label part="field"> (line 232) with one label text ('Size range'). A <label> can only be associated with one labelable control, and neither input has aria-label/aria-labelledby. Screen readers announce both thumbs with the same (or no) name, so a user cannot tell the start (minimum) thumb from the end (maximum) thumb. Give each input a distinct accessible name (e.g. aria-label 'Minimum'/'Maximum') and stop wrapping two controls in one label.

Not a true range slider: the two inputs are stacked vertically in a grid (part="ranges", gap 0.35rem, lines 207-210, 241-260) as two independent full-width sliders. There is no shared track with a highlighted fill between the start and end thumbs, which is the defining visual of a Box range control. It reads as two separate sliders rather than one range.

Value pill breaks in dark mode. Line 200 mixes the brand token with a hardcoded 'white 88%': color-mix(in srgb, var(--boe-token-surface-surface-brand) 12%, white 88%). In a dark theme this stays a light pill, ignoring surface tokens. Use a theme-aware surface token (e.g. item-surface-selected) instead of hardcoded white.

start/end are never snapped to step and are not re-clamped when min/max change. clamp() (lines 119-124) only bounds to min/max and does no step quantization, and syncValue applies no step rounding, so programmatic set start=33 with step=10 keeps 33. Also changing the min/max attributes does not re-clamp startInternal/endInternal, leaving values out of the new range until the next drag.

Only a custom 'value-changed' event is dispatched (line 150), and only on user input — not on programmatic start/end setter changes. There is no 'change'/'input' event and the event name diverges from platform conventions, making it harder to integrate with form tooling. Consider a standard event name and firing on committed changes.

No hover or active/pressed styling on the thumbs — only accent-color and a focus-visible outline (lines 220-224) plus a disabled style (lines 226-230) exist. There is also no error state hook. The thumb gives no pointer-hover affordance beyond cursor:pointer.

Redundant double render on programmatic set. The start/end setters (lines 87-101) both setAttribute and call render(); the setAttribute then triggers attributeChangedCallback which re-parses and calls render() again, so each programmatic assignment renders twice.

summary: A functional but shallow dual-input range control: it uses Box brand/text tokens and has focus-visible and disabled states, but it is two stacked independent sliders rather than a true range with a filled track, and a serious bug tears down the actively-dragged input on every step via the setAttribute→attributeChangedCallback→render loop. Accessibility is weak: two inputs share one label with no distinct accessible names.

A competent star-rating with radiogroup semantics, roving tabindex, and token-driven styling, but a full innerHTML re-render on every interaction destroys the focused element without restoring focus, breaking keyboard navigation, and several colors/tokens are hardcoded or undefined.

issues.description: render() rewrites shadowRoot.innerHTML entirely on every updateValue() (click and every arrow-key press), destroying and recreating all star buttons. The previously focused button is removed and focus is never restored (no activeElement capture / .focus() after render), so focus falls to document.body. After the first ArrowRight/ArrowLeft the keydown listener on the recreated control no longer has focus, making continuous keyboard navigation effectively non-functional.

Line 244-245 references --boe-token-surface-item-surface-hover, which is not defined in the token set (box-defaults.ts has SurfaceSurfaceHover and SurfaceItemSurfaceSelected but no SurfaceItemSurfaceHover). Only the hardcoded #eef4fb fallback ever renders, so the hover styling is not actually token-driven.

Hardcoded non-token colors: the empty-star color rgba(82, 96, 109, 0.45) (line 219) and the control box-shadow values rgba(255,255,255,0.82) and rgba(15,23,42,0.04) (lines 202-203) bypass the token system entirely; the empty state should use a neutral text/stroke token.

No cumulative hover preview. Hovering star 4 only highlights that single star (:hover on one button) rather than filling stars 1-4, which is the expected affordance for a rating control and how users judge the value they are about to select. There is also no active/pressed state distinct from hover.

Double render on property assignment: set value() calls this.render() (line 60) and also setAttribute('value', ...) which triggers attributeChangedCallback -> render() (line 77), rendering twice for a single property set. Combined with full innerHTML replacement this is wasteful on every keystroke.

Not form-associated: the element does not use ElementInternals / static formAssociated, so despite being a form control it contributes no value to an enclosing <form> and cannot participate in form submission or validation.

Spacing/radius and label typography drift from Blueprint: raw rem values are used instead of spacing tokens, and border-radius of 1rem (control) / 0.95rem (stars) is very rounded versus Box's smaller radii; the label is force-uppercased with letter-spacing 0.08em and font-weight 700, which is not the standard Blueprint field-label style.

Arrow-Left/Down from value 1 sets value to 0 (unselected) via the valueInternal <= 1 ? 0 branch, allowing the radiogroup to be arrowed into a no-selection state, which is atypical radiogroup keyboard behavior and can leave no aria-checked radio.

summary: A reasonably structured, XSS-safe review-queue card that correctly uses most --boe-token-* variables and dispatches sensible events, but it is undercooked on interaction states (no hover/active/selected/disabled) and has a real API bug: overriding HTMLElement's native `title`. Solid foundation, not yet Box production quality.

issues.description: The `title` getter/setter (lines 132-138) overrides HTMLElement's built-in `title` property and reflects to the `title` attribute. Setting a title therefore paints a native OS tooltip on the host element, and any consumer-set `title` for a genuine tooltip collides with the component's data model. Use a namespaced attribute like `item-title`/`heading` instead of the reserved `title`.

This is presented as a selectable queue item (a `[part=select]` button that emits a `selected` event, and the design system defines SurfaceItemSurfaceSelected #f2f7fd), yet there is no `selected` attribute, no visual selected state, and no aria-pressed/aria-current. The card cannot show which item is active in a queue.

No hover, active/pressed, or disabled states are implemented anywhere. Only focus-visible exists (lines 408-412). The tokens SurfaceSurfaceHover, SurfaceSurfaceBrandHover, and SurfaceSurfaceBrandPressed are defined but unused, so the select button and action buttons give no pointer feedback and actions cannot be disabled.

Every attributeChangedCallback triggers a full `shadowRoot.innerHTML =` re-render (lines 144-146, 181-448), which destroys and recreates all nodes and re-attaches listeners on each change. Any attribute update while the select or an action button is focused silently drops keyboard focus, hurting keyboard/AT users. Prefer targeted DOM updates or preserving focus.

The assignee avatar uses a hardcoded gradient `linear-gradient(180deg, #2b7de9 0%, #0057c2 100%)` (line 332) and the focus ring uses hardcoded `rgba(0, 97, 213, 0.28)` (line 410) instead of --boe-token-surface-surface-brand. These hardcoded blues bypass the token system and won't follow theme (e.g. box-dark).

The item title is rendered as `<div part="title">` (line 418) with no heading semantics (h2/h3), so a queue of these produces no document outline for screen-reader navigation. The status/priority/due-date pills are also purely visual spans with no programmatic association to the title.
The brand focus ring at 0.28 alpha (line 410) is a faint 2px outline rather than a strong, clearly visible Blueprint brand focus ring, weakening the keyboard focus affordance.

Action `tone` only styles the `primary` case (line 390); any other tone value (e.g. `danger`/`destructive`) silently renders as a neutral button with no visual differentiation, so semantically dangerous actions look identical to safe ones.

Spacing values are off the Blueprint 4/8px rhythm (e.g. 0.95rem, 1.05rem, 0.28rem, 0.72rem at lines 245-246, 291, 386), producing slightly irregular vertical rhythm rather than a consistent spacing scale.
The `[part="meta"]` container is always emitted (line 424) even when no due-date is set, leaving an empty flex row; minor but it is dead markup when dueDateMarkup is empty.

Toolbar buttons are toggle controls (bold/italic/lists) but have no active/selected state: they never reflect document.queryCommandState, carry no aria-pressed, and there is no pressed CSS. A user cannot tell whether the current selection is bold or in a list. This is the single most important missing state for a rich-text toolbar (buttons at lines 288-300; applyCommand at 112-120 fires the command but reads back nothing).

No error/invalid state at all. Despite being a form field wrapped in a <fieldset>, there is no invalid/error attribute, no aria-invalid, and no error styling/message slot. Sibling form fields expose error affordances; this one cannot signal validation failure.

Invalid design token: line 234 uses var(--boe-token-surface-item-surface-hover, #eef4fb) for the button hover background, but no such token exists in src/foundations/tokens (registry only defines SurfaceItemSurfaceSelected and SurfaceSurfaceHover). The hover color therefore always resolves to the hardcoded #eef4fb fallback and won't theme with box-dark.

Over-decorated relative to Box's flat Blueprint aesthetic: stacked linear-gradients on surface, toolbar, tool-button, and editor (lines 184-247) plus hardcoded decorative shadows (rgba(255,255,255,0.82), rgba(15,23,42,0.04)). Box production surfaces are largely flat with single token fills; the gradients and non-tokenized rgba shadows read as bespoke, not Blueprint.

Radii and spacing are ad hoc and oversized: border-radius values of 1.05rem, 1rem, 0.95rem, 0.9rem (~15-17px) across the surface/toolbar/editor/buttons are inconsistent with each other and far larger than Box's typical 4-8px component radii. Spacing (0.9rem, 0.6rem, 1rem) is hardcoded rather than drawn from a spacing scale/token.

Editor body text is not tokenized: :host uses color: inherit and the editor never sets var(--boe-token-text-text, #222222). Typed content color depends entirely on the host page rather than the design-system text token, unlike sibling fields (e.g. text-area sets --boe-token-text on its input).
No :active/pressed visual on tool buttons. The transition declares transform (line 229) but there is no :active rule to drive it, so buttons have no click feedback.

role="toolbar" (line 287) is declared but the WAI-ARIA toolbar keyboard pattern (roving tabindex with Left/Right arrow navigation, single tab stop) is not implemented — all four buttons sit in the tab order and arrow keys do nothing. Either implement the roving-tabindex pattern or drop the toolbar role.

Placeholder relies on [part="editor"]:empty::before (lines 255-258). contentEditable often leaves a stray
or empty

after the user types then deletes everything, making :empty false, so the placeholder disappears while the field appears empty.

value reflects raw innerHTML into the value attribute on every keystroke (handleEditorInput, lines 107-108), writing full HTML markup into a DOM attribute continuously. This is heavy, pollutes the DOM, and — via attributeChangedCallback — triggers a redundant second syncValue on property sets (set value at 71-75 also calls setAttribute then syncValue).

Not form-associated: no static formAssociated / ElementInternals, so the field's value never participates in a native <form> submission despite presenting as a form control. Also only a custom "value-changed" event is emitted; there is no standard input/change signal.

Relies on the deprecated document.execCommand (line 118) for all formatting. It is guarded with optional chaining but is non-standard, being removed from browsers, and silently no-ops where unsupported, leaving toolbar buttons inert with no fallback.

summary: A functional contentEditable rich-text field with solid base ARIA (textbox/toolbar roles, labels, disabled handling) and good token usage in places, but it diverges from Box's flat Blueprint look (gradients, oversized inconsistent radii), references a nonexistent hover token, and omits critical states: no toolbar toggle/active state (aria-pressed + queryCommandState), no error state, and no :active feedback. Deprecated execCommand and non-form-associated value round out a mid-tier component.

A cleanly styled radio-group picker with correct token usage and solid attribute reflection, but it implements only a fraction of the ARIA radiogroup keyboard contract and rebuilds its entire shadow DOM on every selection, dropping focus. State coverage is thin.

issues.description: role="radiogroup"/role="radio" is declared but the required keyboard interaction is missing: there is no keydown handler for Arrow keys/Home/End to move selection between radios, and no roving tabindex. Every view is a native <button>, so all radios are in the Tab order (contrary to the radiogroup pattern where only the checked radio is tabbable and arrows move focus/selection). Keyboard users cannot operate this as a radio group.

Selecting a view calls this.setAttribute("value", nextValue) (line 204), which fires attributeChangedCallback -> render() (lines 61-66, 91), replacing the entire shadowRoot.innerHTML. The just-clicked button is destroyed and focus is lost after every selection — a focus-management regression on interaction.

State coverage is minimal: only :focus-visible (lines 152-155) and [data-selected="true"] (lines 147-150) are styled. There is no hover, :active/pressed, disabled, error, or loading treatment, and no disabled attribute/property support at all. Interactive buttons give no hover affordance.

The value property setter (lines 42-46) calls setAttribute("value", ...) AND render(); setAttribute triggers attributeChangedCallback which sets valueInternal again and calls render() a second time. Every programmatic value assignment renders twice redundantly.

No empty state: when views is [] (or the JSON fails to parse and falls back to []), render produces an empty [part="views"] container with just the label and no placeholder/message, leaving a bare box.

Spacing, radius, and font sizes are hardcoded rem literals (padding 1rem 1.1rem, gap 0.9rem/0.75rem, border-radius 1rem/0.9rem, font-size 0.8rem/0.88rem) rather than Blueprint spacing/radius tokens, so rhythm is not driven by the design-token system even though color tokens are.

The group's visible label is a plain <div part="label"> (line 172) and is not programmatically associated with the radiogroup; the group name is duplicated via aria-label on the section (line 171). Using aria-labelledby pointing at the label element would avoid the duplicated string and keep them in sync.

summary: A competent, token-driven search field with clean hover/focus/active/disabled states and meaningful events, but undermined by a full innerHTML re-render that destroys focus (notably after Clear), interactive buttons nested inside a <label>, and inconsistent value reflection during typing.

issues.description: render() rewrites shadowRoot.innerHTML wholesale on every attribute/value change. If value is set programmatically while the input is focused (or any observed attribute changes mid-typing), the <input> element is destroyed and recreated, losing keyboard focus and cursor/selection position. This makes the component brittle for controlled/reactive usage.

clear() sets this.value = "" which triggers a full render(), recreating the Clear button that was just activated. Keyboard focus is dropped to <body> after clearing instead of being restored to the input or a sensible control — a focus-management regression for keyboard users.

Both the Search and Clear <button>s are placed inside the wrapping <label part="field">. A label implicitly associates with its first labelable descendant (the input) and forwards click activation to it, so clicking the buttons also drives the label/input; nesting interactive controls in a <label> is invalid/ambiguous and should be restructured (associate the label to the input via for/id and move the buttons outside).

Value reflection is inconsistent: the value setter calls setAttribute('value', ...), but the input 'input' handler updates only valueInternal and never reflects to the attribute. During user typing getAttribute('value') stays stale, contradicting the reflection convention the setter establishes.

type="search" renders the browser's native clear (X) affordance in addition to the custom 'Clear' button, giving two clear controls with divergent behavior (native X does not fire the component's 'clear'/'value-changed' events). No role="search" landmark wraps the field either.
The value setter calls setAttribute('value', nextValue) AND render() directly; setAttribute already triggers attributeChangedCallback -> render(), so every programmatic value set renders twice.

The design system defines a dedicated SurfaceSearchSurface token (#f4f4f4) intended for search inputs, but the component ignores it and instead builds a custom white gradient shell, deviating from the intended Blueprint search surface.

The field label uses uppercase + 0.08em letter-spacing + font-weight 700, which does not match Box Blueprint form-label styling (sentence-case, medium weight). It also renders no search/magnifier icon that Box search fields typically include.
The component is not form-associated (no ElementInternals, no name attribute), so it cannot participate in native <form> submission or reset despite semantically being a search input.

No interaction states at all on the interactive controls. [part="action"] and [part="filter-chip"] are real <button>s but the stylesheet (lines 251-277) defines no :hover, :active, or :disabled rules. The design system exposes SurfaceSurfaceBrandHover / SurfaceSurfaceBrandPressed and StrokeStrokeHover tokens that go unused, so buttons feel dead on pointer interaction.

No :focus-visible brand focus ring anywhere. Keyboard users tabbing through the action buttons and filter chips get only the UA default outline (often removed by shadow-root resets), so there is no visible Blueprint focus indicator. There is no error, loading, or selected treatment either.

Filter-removal buttons have no meaningful accessible name. The chip's text is the filter label (e.g. "Type: PDF") and the removal affordance is a CSS ::after " ×" (line 274) which is not read by AT. A screen-reader user hears "Type: PDF, button" with no indication that activating it removes the filter. Each chip needs an aria-label like "Remove filter: Type: PDF".

The <section part="header"> (line 279) is an unlabeled landmark region and the result count is not announced on change. When result-count updates dynamically there is no aria-live region, so screen-reader users are not told the count changed. Consider aria-labelledby on the section pointing at the label, and role="status"/aria-live on the count.

Primary action hardcodes color: #fff (line 265) instead of the available --boe-token-text-text-on-brand token (TextTextOnBrand), so inverse text color won't follow the active design system / dark theme. The color-mix for --_obp-brand-soft also mixes toward literal white rather than a surface token.

Non-primary action tones are silently dropped: data-tone defaults to "neutral" (line 176) and any tone other than "primary" gets the same neutral styling (only [data-tone="primary"] is styled). A caller passing tone: "danger" or "subtle" gets no visual differentiation.
Count label is always pluralized: `${this.resultCount} results` (line 283) renders "1 results" and "0 results" without singular handling.

The dispatched event name "action" (line 116) is extremely generic for a global bubbling+composed event and risks colliding with other components' events; a namespaced name (e.g. "box-search-header-action") would be safer. Reflection and observedAttributes are otherwise correctly implemented.

summary: A functional, token-aware header pattern with clean attribute reflection and meaningful events, but it ships essentially no interaction states (no hover/active/focus-visible/disabled) and has notable a11y gaps around unlabeled filter-removal buttons and undeclared dynamic-count updates.

issues.description: The component exposes `title` as a content attribute (observedAttributes + getter/setter) as an alias for `heading`. `title` is a global HTML attribute that renders a native browser tooltip on hover over the whole element. Setting `title="Members"` (as the pattern implies) causes an unwanted tooltip and conflicts with the intended semantics. Use only `heading` (or a namespaced attr) and drop the `title` alias.

The heading is hardcoded as `<h2>` (line 144) with no way to set the heading level or aria-level. Sections nested at different depths will produce an invalid/inconsistent document outline. Expose a `level`/`heading-level` attribute to render h1-h6 (or set aria-level) so the region fits its surrounding hierarchy.

`render()` rebuilds the entire shadow DOM via `shadowRoot.innerHTML = ...` on every `attributeChangedCallback` (lines 65-67, 80-155). This re-parses the `<style>` block and destroys/recreates the `<slot>` elements on each attribute change, forcing slot re-distribution and unnecessary reflow. Build the DOM once and update only the affected text nodes (eyebrow/title/description) on subsequent changes.

Empty-string heading defeats the default fallback. `get heading()` uses `getAttribute("heading") ?? getAttribute("title") ?? "Section"` (line 30); an explicit `heading=""` returns `""` (not null), so the `??` chain does not fall back, yielding an empty `<h2 id=box-section-title>` and a region whose `aria-labelledby` resolves to an empty accessible name. Guard against empty/whitespace strings before falling back.

The `description` text (line 78) is rendered but not linked to the section via `aria-describedby`, so assistive tech does not associate it with the region. Give the description a stable id and reference it with `aria-describedby` on the `<section>` when present.

All spacing, font sizes, and rhythm are hardcoded rem literals (e.g. gap 0.85rem, title 1.15rem, eyebrow 0.78rem at lines 90-133). Only two color tokens are consumed. The design system currently ships no spacing/radius scale, but the magic numbers are not derived from any shared rhythm, so cross-component spacing consistency is not guaranteed.

No focus-visible or interactive states exist, which is acceptable for a layout-only region, but the `actions` slot commonly holds buttons; the header provides no visual affordance (e.g. divider/hover treatment) and offers nothing to distinguish an empty vs populated actions area, so an empty actions slot still reserves flex layout space.

summary: A competent layout-only titled region that correctly uses the two available text color tokens, escapes HTML, and reflects its attributes with a labelled `<section>` region. It is held back by the `title` attribute colliding with the native HTML tooltip, a hardcoded h2 heading level, a full-shadow-DOM rebuild on every attribute change, and an empty-string heading edge case.

A competent radiogroup-pattern segmented control with correct token usage, roving tabindex, and arrow-key navigation, but it ships an unfinished "attached" layout (radii never adjust), a dark-mode-hostile glossy treatment built from hardcoded white blends, and a subtle focus-selector escaping bug.

issues.description: The 'attached' vs 'separated' layout feature is incomplete. render() computes data-position="first|middle|last|only" on every button (lines 141-148) and sets data-layout on segments, but NO CSS rule references [data-position] or [part="segment"][data-layout]. The only layout style is [part="control"][data-layout="attached"]{gap:0} (line 193). So 'attached' segments collapse the gap yet keep individual 0.55rem border-radius on every button, producing overlapping rounded corners instead of the expected first-rounded / middle-square / last-rounded grouping. The position computation is dead code.

The glossy treatment is hardcoded for light mode and breaks the dark theme (box-dark tokens exist). The control background gradient mixes each surface token with literal white (e.g. '...surface-secondary 88%, white 12%'), the segment hover mixes '...surface-hover 60%, white 40%', and both control and selected segment apply inset white highlights rgba(255,255,255,0.82) plus a rgba(15,23,42,0.08) drop shadow (lines 182-227). On dark surfaces these white blends and light highlights invert the intended depth, so the component won't respect --boe-token dark values despite referencing them.

escapeHtml() is misused as a CSS-selector escape in moveSelection(). Line 123 builds `[part="segment"][data-value="${escapeHtml(nextValue)}"]`, but the browser stores the un-escaped attribute value in the DOM, so a value containing & < > " ' (e.g. 'a&b') is rendered as data-value="a&b", parsed back to 'a&b', and the selector `[data-value="a&b"]` then fails to match. Result: after arrow-key navigation the newly selected button never receives focus for such values. Should use CSS.escape on the raw value instead.

Keyboard support is incomplete for the radiogroup pattern. keydown (lines 269-280) handles only ArrowRight/Down/Left/Up. Home and End (jump to first/last option) are not implemented, and there is no wrap-boundary announcement. Arrow navigation also uses selection-follows-focus which immediately mutates value on each arrow press; acceptable for radios, but combined with the missing Home/End it's below the full WAI-ARIA radiogroup keyboard contract.

No active/pressed state is styled. There is :hover, :focus-visible (3px brand ring), [data-selected="true"], and :disabled, but no :active rule on [part="segment"], so pressing a segment gives no tactile press feedback before the selection settles.

Redundant double render on programmatic value changes. The value setter (lines 79-83) calls setAttribute("value", ...) AND render(); setAttribute triggers attributeChangedCallback (lines 89-95) which also calls render(). Every .value assignment thus rebuilds the entire shadow DOM (full innerHTML replacement plus re-attaching click/keydown listeners on all segments) twice. Wasteful and re-creates the <style> block on each keystroke.

The component dispatches a non-standard 'value-changed' CustomEvent (lines 114-120, 259-265) rather than a conventional 'change' event, and dispatches nothing on programmatic value changes. Consumers expecting the platform-typical change/input semantics of a form control must special-case this name; consider also exposing form-association (ElementInternals) since it presents as a single-select control.

The change handler (lines 163-174) calls this.setAttribute("value", nextValue), which fires attributeChangedCallback -> render() (lines 75-81, 83), replacing the entire shadow DOM innerHTML — including the very <select> the user just interacted with — in the middle of the change event. This destroys and recreates the native control on every selection, risking focus loss, dropdown flicker, and detached-element issues. The public value setter (lines 27-31) is worse: it calls both render() AND setAttribute, causing a double full re-render.

No error/invalid state exists at all. There is no error/invalid attribute, no aria-invalid, no --boe-token status-error styling, and no error-message slot/text. Form selects routinely need validation display; this is entirely missing.

Value/option desync: if valueInternal (e.g. from the value attribute) does not match any option, line 162 sets select.value to a non-existent value, so the native <select> silently falls back to its first option while valueInternal retains the stale string. The displayed selection and this.value then disagree with no reconciliation. There is also no placeholder/empty-option handling for the unset case.

The dropdown chevron SVG (line 124) hardcodes stroke='%2352606d' — a cool blue-grey that is not a --boe-token-* variable, so it ignores theme/dark-mode and the warm-neutral Blueprint palette. Combine with arbitrary radius (0.7rem) and spacing values not drawn from a Blueprint spacing scale, plus a decorative linear-gradient field background (lines 125-129) that is not typical of Box's flat Blueprint fields.

No support for required or error semantics: no aria-invalid, no aria-required, and no aria-describedby linking to an error/help message. The label is a custom uppercase eyebrow (font-weight 700, letter-spacing, text-transform uppercase, lines 108-114) rather than a standard field label; while the wrapping <label> provides an accessible name, there is no explicit for/id association or required-field indicator.

Not form-associated: no static formAssociated / ElementInternals, no name attribute, so the component's value will not be submitted with a native <form> and cannot participate in form validation. observedAttributes (line 13) also omits placeholder, required, error/invalid, and name.

Dispatches a custom 'value-changed' event (lines 167-173) but never re-emits a standard 'change'/'input' event, so consumers expecting native form-control events must special-case this element. The event name convention also differs from a plain 'change'.

render() rebuilds the whole shadow innerHTML (lines 95-158) on every attribute change and every selection, re-parsing the options JSON (lines 53-65) and re-attaching listeners each time. Functionally correct (old listeners are GC'd with the replaced DOM) but wasteful and the root cause of the mid-interaction re-render problem.

summary: A thin wrapper over a native <select> with solid token usage, hover/focus/disabled states, and XSS-safe escaping, but it lacks any error/required/placeholder handling, is not form-associated, and re-renders its entire shadow DOM mid-change which can disrupt the live control and desync value from displayed selection.

A reasonably token-driven, well-escaped composite panel, but it is undermined by a broken (invisible) focus indicator, hardcoded Box-blue rgba values that won't re-theme in dark mode, a primary-button styling bug, and thin accessibility semantics. Solid API surface with reflection and events, but not production quality.

issues.description: Interactive elements (shared-link-url, collaborator, and implicitly actions) set `outline: none` on `:focus-visible` and only nudge the border color with a very faint alpha (e.g. rgba(0,97,213,0.24) at lines 323-327, 400-404). Keyboard users get no perceptible brand focus ring, failing WCAG 2.4.7. There is no visible focus indicator replacement.

Multiple hardcoded Box-blue values bypass tokens: rgba(0,97,213,0.24)/0.22 hover borders (lines 325, 402) and rgba(0,97,213,0.12) avatar background (line 412). These do not re-theme in dark mode (where surface-brand becomes #4596ff), producing a light-blue avatar/hover on dark surfaces. Should be color-mix over --boe-token-surface-surface-brand.

Primary action bug: `[part="action"][data-tone="primary"]` sets `border-color: var(--boe-token-text-text, #1f1e1b)` (line 455) — a near-black border around a blue button — and `color: white` hardcoded instead of --boe-token-text-text-on-brand (line 456). Looks like a copy/paste error; the border should match the brand fill or be transparent.

The `[part="action"]` buttons have no hover, focus-visible, active, or disabled styling at all (only base rules at lines 441-457). Actions are the primary interactive affordance yet give no visual feedback on hover/press/keyboard focus, and there is no disabled/loading state supported anywhere in the component.

No semantic heading or region labeling. The panel title is a plain `<div part="title">` (line 461), not an h2/role=heading, and the outer `<section part="panel">` has no aria-labelledby tying it to the title. The shared-link URL is a `<button>` wrapping the raw URL text (line 157) with no aria-label such as 'Copy link', so screen readers announce only the URL with no indication that activating it copies.

Overriding `get/set title` (lines 94-100) collides with the native HTMLElement `title` property/attribute: setting it surfaces a native browser tooltip on the host and conflates the panel heading with the tooltip attribute. A dedicated `heading`/`panel-title` attribute would avoid the collision.

No selected/active state for collaborators. Collaborator rows are buttons that emit `collaborator-selected` (lines 480-489) but there is no `data-selected`/aria-pressed styling to reflect the chosen collaborator, so selection has no visual persistence.

`emitCollaboratorSelected` reconstructs the collaborator from data-* attributes (lines 481-488), silently dropping `initials` (and any future fields). Consumers listening to the event receive a lossy object rather than the original collaborator record.

`render()` rebuilds the entire shadow DOM via innerHTML on every attributeChangedCallback (line 235, 106-108). This discards focus/scroll state on any attribute change and re-attaches all listeners each time; fine for static demos but brittle for live-updating panels.

Spacing, radius, and font-size use arbitrary off-grid values not on a Blueprint 4px rhythm: paddings like 1.1rem/0.95rem/0.72rem, radii 1rem/0.95rem/0.9rem/0.85rem/0.8rem, font-sizes 0.92rem/0.94rem (lines 246-457). There are no --boe-token spacing/radius tokens consumed, so rhythm is inconsistent across sections.

render() blows away shadowRoot.innerHTML and re-creates the <button> plus its click listener on EVERY attributeChangedCallback. Since toggle() sets this.expanded (a reflected attribute), each activation destroys and rebuilds the DOM node. This is the classic innerHTML-in-render anti-pattern: it thrashes the DOM, re-parses a full <style> block per toggle, and requires the manual focus-restore hack at lines 91/164 to avoid dumping keyboard focus to document.body.

The declared CSS transitions never actually play. The `transition: background/border-color/color 140ms` (line 115) and the chevron `transform: scaleX(-1)` flip on state change (lines 139-142) are dead: because the <button> element is fully recreated on every state change (render rebuilds innerHTML), a brand-new node with the new data-expanded value is inserted, so the browser has no from-state to interpolate. The intended animated hamburger flip snaps instantly.

No :active / pressed visual state is defined. Only hover, focus-visible, and disabled are styled. A production toggle button should give pointer feedback on press (e.g. a pressed background/border token) — the component jumps straight from hover to release with no active feedback.

The dispatched event is named "toggle" with bubbles:true and composed:true (lines 75-81). "toggle" is a reserved native DOM event name (fired by <details> and the Popover API). A composed, bubbling custom "toggle" event can collide with host listeners expecting the native event and is easy to mishandle; a namespaced name like "boe:toggle" would be safer.

The focus-visible ring is very faint: box-shadow using surface-surface-brand mixed at only 22% opacity (line 131). Box's brand focus ring is typically a solid, high-contrast 2-3px indicator; at 22% alpha over a white surface this is barely visible and weakens the accessible focus affordance.

aria-label is static ("Toggle sidebar" default, or the fixed `label`) and never changes with state; it stays the same whether collapsed or expanded. While aria-expanded conveys the state, best practice for a disclosure toggle is an action label that reflects intent (e.g. "Collapse navigation" / "Expand navigation"). The label prop cannot express both directions.

Sizing values are off any obvious Blueprint spacing scale: inline/block-size 2.1rem, border-radius 0.6rem, icon 1.1rem (lines 107-108, 111, 119-120). These read as hand-tuned pixel-to-rem conversions rather than tokenized spacing/radius steps, so the control won't stay in rhythm if the design system's radius/size scale changes.

The comment at line 139 says "Rotate the chevron" but the icon is a three-line hamburger, and the state indicator is scaleX(-1) — which only mirrors the asymmetric shorter third line rather than rotating a directional chevron. The visual affordance for which direction the next activation moves the sidebar is weak and the comment mismatches the actual markup.

summary: A clean, accessible disclosure button with correct native-button semantics, full attribute reflection, and valid --boe-token-* usage, but let down by an innerHTML-rebuild-on-every-change render loop that makes its declared transitions and chevron animation dead code and forces a manual focus-restore hack. Missing active state, a faint focus ring, and a native-name-colliding \"toggle\" event keep it below production bar.

issues.description: HTML/attribute-injection bug: render() interpolates this.width and this.height directly into an inline style attribute inside a template literal assigned to shadowRoot.innerHTML (line 81: style="...width:${this.width};height:${this.height};"). These values come straight from unescaped user-controllable attributes. A value such as width='16px" aria-hidden="false">' breaks out of the attribute and injects arbitrary markup into the shadow root. Width/height should be applied via element.style.setProperty on a created node, or validated/escaped, not string-concatenated into innerHTML.

The shimmer highlight is built with color-mix(... white 12%) hardcoded (line 56). box-dark.ts exists, so the component renders in dark mode too, and mixing toward literal white produces a wrong, washed-out highlight on dark surfaces instead of a token-driven lighter neutral. The highlight stop should derive from a surface/stroke token, not white.

border-radius is hardcoded to 0.5rem (line 51) rather than using a Blueprint radius token (e.g. a --boe-token-radius-* variable). This bypasses the design system's radius scale and cannot follow theme overrides.

API is limited to width/height only. A production skeleton component typically exposes shape/variant (text line, circle for avatars, rect), a radius attribute, and often a count for multi-line text blocks. None of these exist, so consumers must manually size every skeleton and cannot express a circular avatar placeholder without overriding the part's border-radius.

aria-hidden="true" is placed on the inner span (line 82) rather than on the host element. The <box-skeleton> host itself remains exposed in the accessibility tree; hiding only the shadow child is sloppy. Prefer setting aria-hidden on the host (or role="presentation") so the decorative placeholder is fully removed from AT, and there is no attribute/hook to coordinate a loading announcement (aria-busy) with the region it fills.

render() rewrites the entire shadowRoot.innerHTML (style block + keyframes + node) on every attributeChangedCallback (lines 33-35, 42-84). Changing width or height destroys and recreates the animated node, restarting the shimmer animation and thrashing the style parser. Building the DOM once and only updating inline size on subsequent changes would be more correct and avoid animation resets.

summary: A minimal, mostly-functional skeleton that correctly uses surface/stroke tokens and honors prefers-reduced-motion, but it has a real HTML-injection escaping bug from interpolating width/height into innerHTML, a hardcoded white shimmer highlight that breaks in dark mode, a hardcoded radius, and a thin API lacking shape/variant/count options.

A clean, functional slider that leans on a native range input for solid accessibility and keyboard support, but visual polish is thin (hardcoded white breaks dark mode, no custom track/thumb) and the value is never clamped/stepped, letting the pill diverge from the thumb.

issues.description: set value (line 80-85) normalizes only for finiteness, never clamps to [min,max] or snaps to step. Setting value=999 with max=100 makes the value pill (part="value") show "999" while the native input thumb clamps to 100, producing a visible mismatch between the displayed number and the slider position.

The value pill background hardcodes white: color-mix(in srgb, var(--boe-token-surface-surface-brand,#0061d5) 12%, white 88%) (line 151). This is not theme-aware — under box-dark it renders a near-white pill on a dark surface. Use a surface token (e.g. --boe-token-surface-item-surface-selected) instead of literal white.

syncValue (line 99-112), the handler for user drag/input, updates valueInternal and the pill textContent and dispatches value-changed, but never reflects to the value attribute. After a user interacts, getAttribute('value') and the value property (getter returns valueInternal) disagree — the reflected attribute stays at its initial value, breaking the attribute-reflection convention used everywhere else.

No hover or active/pressed state, and no error/invalid state exists. Only focus-visible (line 165) and disabled (line 170) are styled. There is no way to render a validation error or a pressed thumb, which Blueprint form controls typically support.

Setting the value property causes a double render: set value calls this.setAttribute('value', ...) (line 83) which triggers attributeChangedCallback -> render(), and then set value calls this.render() again (line 84). Every full render also re-parses innerHTML and re-attaches the input listener.

The control does no custom styling of the range track or thumb — it relies solely on the browser default shape via accent-color (line 161). Spacing (0.6rem/0.8rem/0.25rem) and radius (999px) are raw literals rather than Blueprint spacing/radius tokens, so rhythm is not tied to the design system.

The native input gets its accessible name from the wrapping <label part="field"> (line 175), which also contains the value pill span; the computed name therefore includes the numeric value (e.g. "Density 40") rather than just the label. There is also no aria-valuetext for units-aware announcements.

summary: A visually polished spinbutton with good token usage and a solid attribute/reflection API, but it has a real accessibility gap (the visible label is never associated with the input, so the control has no accessible name) and correctness gaps (value is not clamped on initial render or external attribute changes).

issues.description: The visible <span part="label"> (e.g. 'Quota (GB)') is never associated with the <input>. There is no <label for>, aria-labelledby, or aria-label on the input, so the spinbutton has no accessible name — screen readers announce only the value/role. Wire the label via aria-labelledby to an id on the span, or set aria-label from this.label.

clamp() is only applied inside syncValue(). The initial render and any external `value` attribute/property change do NOT clamp: setting value=1000 with max=500 leaves valueInternal=1000, displays 1000, and sets aria-valuenow=1000, violating min/max. Clamp in the value setter and attributeChangedCallback.

Increment/decrement buttons are never disabled at the min/max boundaries. When value equals max (or min) the '+' ('-') button stays active and clickable but does nothing, giving no visual affordance that the limit is reached. Real spinbuttons disable/dim the boundary button.

No error/invalid visual state is implemented. There is no `invalid`/`error` attribute or styling (e.g. --boe-token status error stroke), so out-of-range or invalid entry cannot be surfaced. aria-invalid is also never set.

render() rebuilds the entire shadow DOM innerHTML and re-attaches all listeners on every attributeChangedCallback and on every `value` setter call. The value setter calls render() and then the resulting setAttribute('value') triggers attributeChangedCallback which calls render() again — a redundant double render. It also means an external attribute change while the input is focused destroys and recreates the input, dropping focus and caret position.

role="spinbutton" is explicitly set on a native <input type="number">, which already exposes spinbutton semantics; the manual role plus aria-valuemin/max/now is redundant and can conflict. aria-valuetext is not provided, and PageUp/PageDown large-step keyboard handling is missing (only Arrow/Home/End are handled).

step is not validated: a 0 or negative step attribute is accepted, so increment/decrement can no-op or invert direction. min/max ordering (min > max) is also unchecked, which would make clamp produce surprising values.

Only a custom 'value-changed' event is dispatched; there is no standard 'change'/'input' event and the element is not form-associated (no ElementInternals / name-value participation), so it cannot be submitted natively within a <form>.

Spacing, radius, and control sizing use hardcoded rem values (gap 0.45rem, radius 0.7rem, inline-size 2.25rem/4.5rem) rather than Blueprint spacing/radius tokens, so the component won't track systemic rhythm/radius changes even though colors are correctly tokenized.

The indicator track color is built with color-mix(in srgb, var(--boe-token-surface-surface-brand) 18%, white 82%) — the second color is hardcoded 'white'. In a dark theme the ring track stays light instead of adapting, since it mixes against white rather than a surface token like --boe-token-surface-surface-tertiary. This breaks theme fidelity.

API is minimal: only 'label' is observed/reflected. There is no size variant (e.g. small/medium/large) attribute despite the indicator being a fixed 1.1rem, and no way to render an accessible-only label (label text is always visually rendered). No option to hide the visible label for icon-only usage.

Setting label="" (empty attribute) bypasses the 'Loading' default (getAttribute returns '' which is non-null, so ?? never applies), leaving the role=status region with no accessible name / announcement text. There is also no visually-hidden fallback label when the visible label is undesired.

No size or contextual variants and no error/complete terminal state. reduced-motion is handled (animation slowed to 1.6s) which is good, but the component only expresses a single loading state with no way to signal success/failure completion.

All geometry is hardcoded (indicator 1.1rem, 2.5px border, gap 0.5rem, label font-size 0.86rem, font-weight 600). The non-standard 0.86rem and fixed pixel border don't follow a Blueprint spacing/type scale. (Mitigated by the token set exposing only colors/fonts, no spacing tokens.)

attributeChangedCallback rebuilds the entire shadowRoot innerHTML (style + markup) on every label change, which restarts the CSS rotation animation and re-parses the stylesheet unnecessarily. Updating only the label textContent would avoid the visible animation reset and churn.

summary: A clean, correct, XSS-safe spinner with good baseline a11y (role=status, aria-live, reduced-motion) and correct brand/secondary token usage, but fidelity and API are thin: the track color hardcodes white so it won't adapt to dark themes, there are no size/label-hiding options, and an empty label attribute yields no accessible name.

A minimally-styled layout splitter that reads a stroke token correctly but ships a drag-breaking re-render bug, an unreachable keyboard-inaccessible separator, and no resize events. Functional as a static two-pane grid, but the resizable feature is effectively broken and inaccessible.

issues.description: attributeChangedCallback() unconditionally calls render(), which rebuilds the entire shadow innerHTML. During a drag, pointermove sets this.ratio (line 135), which writes the 'ratio' attribute (line 40), triggering a full re-render that DESTROYS the [part="separator"] element currently holding setPointerCapture. The re-created separator is a fresh node with no capture, so subsequent pointermove/pointerup fire on a detached element and the drag breaks after the first move. It can also leave document.body cursor/userSelect stuck (stopResize never runs on the live node).

The separator has role="separator" + aria-orientation + aria-label but is NOT keyboard-focusable (no tabindex="0") and has zero keyboard handlers. A resizable/focusable window splitter per the ARIA APG must be reachable and support Arrow/Home/End keys. Resize is pointer-only, excluding keyboard and assistive-tech users entirely.

role="separator" that is user-adjustable must expose aria-valuenow, aria-valuemin, and aria-valuemax reflecting the current ratio. None are set, so screen readers cannot announce the split position or that it is adjustable.

No events are dispatched. A resizable component should emit a custom event (e.g. 'box-split-view-resize' / change) when ratio changes so consumers can react or persist the layout. Callers have no way to observe resize other than polling the attribute.

The separator has cursor: col-resize but no hover, focus-visible, or active styling. Sibling components (e.g. sidebar-toggle-button) render a brand focus ring via box-shadow with --boe-token-surface-surface-brand; the separator gives no visual affordance on hover or during an active drag.

Full shadow innerHTML re-render on every attribute change re-parses styles and re-attaches four pointer listeners each time. Beyond the drag bug, this is wasteful and means listeners accumulate/reset unpredictably; the render should build DOM once and update only grid-template-columns / aria values on ratio changes.

Only one token is consumed (--boe-token-stroke-stroke for the 1px separator line). No surface/background separation between panes, no radius, no Blueprint spacing rhythm, and no brand accent on interaction, so the component looks unstyled beyond a hairline rule.

grid-template-columns uses minmax(180px, ratioPercent) for the primary pane; when the container is narrow the 180px floor can exceed ratioPercent, inverting the intended min/max and causing layout overflow with the fixed 12px separator track.

summary: A competent token-driven switch with correct brand palette, ARIA role/state, and native-button keyboard support, but it re-writes shadow innerHTML on every toggle (losing focus), never associates the description with the control, and offers no form participation.

issues.description: render() overwrites shadowRoot.innerHTML on every toggle/attributeChange (lines 114, 230-232), so the currently-focused <button> is destroyed and re-created. After toggling via keyboard, focus is lost to <body>, breaking keyboard flow and focus management.

The switch button uses aria-label (line 215), which overrides its subtree, so the visible description rendered inside the button (lines 223-226) is never exposed to assistive tech. There is no aria-describedby linking the description part to the control.
No form participation: the component uses no ElementInternals/formAssociated, no hidden input, and exposes no name/value. A switch cannot submit its state in a native form, limiting real-world use.
Only a non-standard 'checked-changed' CustomEvent is dispatched (line 92). No standard 'change' (or 'input') event is emitted, so form libraries and generic listeners expecting change won't react.

trackPart/thumbPart compute 'track track-checked'/'thumb thumb-checked' (lines 111-112) but no CSS or consumer references the *-checked part names; styling is driven entirely by the data-checked attribute, making these strings dead code.

set checked (lines 27-36) calls setAttribute('checked') which triggers attributeChangedCallback -> render(), then calls render() again explicitly, causing a redundant double render on every programmatic set.
The :active pressed color is only applied to the checked track (line 174); there is no active/pressed feedback for the unchecked track, so the off-state press feels unresponsive.

Elevation shadows are hardcoded rgba(15,23,42,...) (lines 158, 184-185) rather than design tokens, and the focus-visible ring is a soft 34% color-mix (line 138) instead of the solid brand focus ring Blueprint uses, reducing contrast.

When disabled, both the native disabled attribute (non-focusable) and aria-disabled="true" are set (lines 216-218); aria-disabled is redundant on an already-disabled native button and can be inconsistent if only one is toggled.

summary: A competent segmented-control-style tabs component with solid roving-tabindex keyboard support and token-based theming, but it leans heavily on hardcoded rgba/white color-mix values that break in dark mode, references a non-existent hover token, and omits active/disabled states plus tabpanel association.

issues.description: The hover style references --boe-token-surface-item-surface-hover (lines 218-219), which does not exist in the token registry (box-defaults.ts has SurfaceItemSurfaceSelected but no ItemSurfaceHover). It therefore always resolves to the hardcoded fallback #eef4fb, meaning the hover color is effectively hardcoded and not theme-driven.

Styling is not dark-mode aware despite box-dark.ts existing. Fixed light-mode literals like box-shadow: inset 0 1px 0 rgba(255,255,255,0.82) (lines 151, 209), the 0 6px 12px rgba(15,23,42,...) drop shadow (line 210), and every gradient mixing tokens with literal 'white' (lines 148-149, 161-162, 204-206, 218-219) will render as bright highlights and washed-out surfaces on a dark surface token.
No :active / pressed state is implemented, and the SurfaceSurfaceBrandPressed token (#004eaa) that exists for exactly this purpose is unused. Tabs give no press feedback beyond the transition.
No disabled state exists at the component or per-option level. Options are only {label, value}; there is no way to disable an individual tab, and no disabled visual/skip-in-keyboard-nav handling.

role="tab" buttons have no aria-controls and there is no associated tabpanel (role="tabpanel"/id) anywhere. A true tabs widget should link each tab to its panel; as-is it is a tablist with no panel semantics, which is misleading to assistive tech if used as actual tabs.

Setting the value property (lines 66-70) calls render() AND setAttribute('value',...), and the setAttribute triggers attributeChangedCallback (lines 76-82) which calls render() again — a double synchronous render on every programmatic value set.

Every selection change (including each ArrowLeft/Right/Home/End keypress under the selection-follows-focus model) rebuilds the entire shadow DOM via innerHTML and then restores focus with a queueMicrotask refocus (lines 284-287). This full teardown-per-keystroke plus microtask focus juggling is fragile and inefficient compared to updating aria-selected/tabindex in place.

The value-changed event fires on mere arrow-key focus movement (lines 276-282), not only on explicit activation, so consumers receive a value change on every roving-focus keystroke. Consistent with accordion's naming, but the firing granularity may surprise listeners; there is also no separate distinction between focus and commit.
aria-label defaults to the generic literal "Tabs" (label getter, line 29) when no label is supplied, giving screen-reader users an unhelpful non-descriptive tablist name.

summary: A well-built tag/token input with correct --boe-token-* usage, thoughtful render logic, XSS-safe templating, and a clean event/method API. Held back by a focus-management bug when removing chips by click, no error/invalid state, and no native form participation.

issues.description: Removing a tag by clicking its X button loses keyboard focus. removeTag() -> reflectValue() -> attributeChangedCallback -> render() rebuilds the entire shadow DOM via innerHTML, destroying the clicked button; unlike the Backspace path (handleKeydown calls restoreFocus()), the click handler at line 349 never restores focus, so focus falls to <body>. Keyboard/AT users are stranded after each removal.

No error/invalid state at all. For a form input there is no aria-invalid, no error styling hook, and no way to surface validation (e.g. rejected duplicate, or being at max). addTag() silently returns false on duplicate/at-max with zero visual or programmatic feedback.

Not a form-associated custom element (no static formAssociated / ElementInternals / attachInternals). It cannot participate in native <form> submission or validation, and dispatches only custom tag-added/tag-removed/tags-changed events — no standard 'change' or 'input' event — so consumers must special-case it for forms.

No aria-live/status region. Adding or removing a tag (especially the Backspace-removes-last flow) produces no announcement to screen readers; the visually-hidden hint (line 333) is static instruction text, not a live status, so state changes are silent.

At max the input is fully disabled (line 330, disabled when atMax) and its placeholder is blanked. This removes the field from the tab order and strips context for AT users even though tags can still be removed, making it unclear why input stopped working; a non-disabled read-only/messaging approach would be better.

No active/pressed state on the remove button or control. Only hover and focus-visible are styled (lines 270-277); Box Blueprint components typically show a pressed treatment (e.g. surface-surface-brand-pressed token exists) which is unused here.

Comma-separated paste is not split. Typing/pasting 'a, b, c' then committing passes the whole string to addTag(), which stores it as a single tag containing commas (parseList is only used for the value attribute, not for draft commit at line 356/124). A paste handler or splitting in commitDraft would match the comma-to-commit affordance.
max=0 is treated as unlimited. The max getter (line 72-75) returns Infinity unless raw > 0, so max="0" silently means no limit rather than blocking all tags, which is a surprising edge case.

Spacing, radius, and the uppercase/letter-spaced label are hardcoded rem/px literals (e.g. gap 0.45rem, padding 0.4rem 0.5rem, border-radius 0.7rem, letter-spacing 0.08em) rather than a spacing/radius scale. Color tokens are used well, but the rhythm is ad hoc and the uppercase caps-label styling is a stylistic departure from typical Blueprint field labels.

Every interaction triggers a full shadowRoot.innerHTML rebuild. updateChecklist() and updateAssignee() write JSON/attribute values that fire attributeChangedCallback -> render(), which destroys and recreates the entire DOM. Clicking an assignee or toggling a checkbox therefore blows away the element the user just interacted with, so keyboard focus is lost after every click/toggle. State should be reconciled in place, not re-rendered wholesale.

The assignee-list declares role="listbox" with role="option" children, but the listbox keyboard pattern is not implemented: each option is a native <button> (all are tab stops), there is no roving tabindex, no arrow-key navigation, and no aria-activedescendant. A real listbox needs a single tab stop with Up/Down selection. As written it is neither a proper listbox nor a plain button group, so AT users get a broken/misleading interaction model.

No hover, active/pressed, or disabled states are implemented anywhere. Only focus-visible and the selected (aria-selected) state exist. Tokens for these states already exist and go unused: --boe-token-surface-surface-hover, --boe-token-surface-surface-brand-hover, and --boe-token-surface-surface-brand-pressed. Assignee rows, actions (including the primary action), and checkboxes give no hover/active affordance.

The component uses an attribute/property literally named `title`. This collides with the global HTML `title` attribute: setting title="Contract review" (as the docs example does) also produces a native browser tooltip on the host element, and the getter's default of "Task Assignment" shadows/fights the platform semantics. A namespaced attribute such as `panel-title`/`heading` would avoid the clash.

The panel title is rendered as a plain <div part="title"> with no heading role or level, and the panel <section> is not associated with it via aria-labelledby. Screen-reader users get no landmark/heading to identify the panel, and status/priority/due-date changes are not announced (no aria-live region).

Dark theme is not handled: several values hardcode `white` rather than tokens (color: white on the avatar and primary action, and color-mix(... white 6%/12%/18%) throughout). Under the box-dark token set these white mixes/foregrounds will be wrong. Foregrounds on brand surfaces should use --boe-token-text-text-on-brand.

Spacing and radius use arbitrary, off-scale values (padding 1.1rem/0.28rem/0.72rem/0.85rem, gaps 0.45rem/0.55rem/0.7rem, radius 1rem/0.9rem) instead of a consistent 4px Blueprint rhythm or radius tokens, so vertical rhythm and corner radii are inconsistent across sections.

Reflection convention is inconsistent: currentAssigneeId, dueDate, priority, and status remove their attribute when set to an empty value, but the `message` and `title` setters always call setAttribute even for an empty string, leaving stray empty attributes instead of clearing them.

summary: A reasonably tokenized, XSS-safe panel with good event/reflection API and a working focus ring, but undermined by a full-DOM re-render on every interaction that loses focus, a broken (keyboard-incomplete) listbox pattern, missing hover/active/disabled states, and a native `title` attribute collision.

A competent, token-driven textarea with a proper brand focus ring and clean hover/disabled states, but it lacks any error/validation surface, isn't form-associated, and rebuilds its entire shadow DOM on every attribute change — which loses focus and caret position.

issues.description: render() sets shadowRoot.innerHTML wholesale on every attributeChangedCallback and every value setter call (lines 83-153). Any external property change (e.g. framework re-binding .value, toggling disabled, changing label) destroys and recreates the <textarea>, discarding user focus, caret position, text selection, scroll position, and undo history. A form control should mutate existing nodes, not re-stamp the DOM.

No error/invalid state exists. For a form input there is no error attribute, no error/helper message region, no invalid border/ring styling, and no way to visually communicate validation failure — a core Blueprint field affordance is entirely missing.

No validation/description a11y wiring: no aria-invalid, no aria-describedby linking to error/helper text, and no required/aria-required support. Assistive tech users get no signal about invalid or required state.

Not a form-associated custom element (no static formAssociated/ElementInternals) and has no name attribute, so the value never participates in native <form> submission or reset — a significant gap for a forms-package component.

Value/attribute desync: the input handler (lines 156-166) updates valueInternal and fires the event but never reflects to the value attribute, while the value setter does call setAttribute('value', ...). So getAttribute('value') is stale after typing but fresh after programmatic set — inconsistent reflection that breaks attribute-based state inspection.

Only a non-standard 'value-changed' CustomEvent is dispatched; the component never emits standard 'input'/'change' events, so consumers expecting conventional form events (or event.target.value) must special-case this control.
Missing common textarea attributes: readonly, required, maxlength, minlength, and name are neither observed nor rendered onto the inner <textarea>, limiting real-world usefulness.
The visible label defaults to 'Details' when no label attribute is supplied (line 46), so an unlabeled instance silently renders a misleading fixed label rather than warning or omitting it.

No property-upgrade guard (no deletion/reset of pre-upgrade instance properties). If a framework assigns .value before the element is defined/upgraded, the own property shadows the accessor and the initial value is lost.

Spacing and radius are arbitrary magic numbers (gap 0.45rem, padding 0.6rem/0.85rem, border-radius 0.7rem) rather than a shared spacing/radius scale, and the gradient background (lines 116-121) is a decorative flourish that diverges from Box's typically flat white input surface.
