summary: A competent single-input text field with clean, valid token usage and a proper brand focus ring, but it lacks an error/invalid state entirely and rebuilds its whole shadow DOM on every attribute change, which destroys the live input and drops focus/cursor. API surface is thin (no error/required/name/type, nonstandard-only event).

issues.description: No error/invalid state exists at all. There is no `error`/`invalid` attribute, no error-message rendering, no error stroke color (SurfaceStatusSurfaceError #ed3757 / StrokeStrokeError tokens are available and used elsewhere), and no aria-invalid. For a form text field this is a core missing state.

render() blows away the entire shadow DOM via `shadowRoot.innerHTML = ...` on every attributeChangedCallback and every value setter. This recreates the <input>, so any programmatic attribute/value change while the field is focused destroys the live input element, losing focus and cursor position. A live text field should patch attributes on a persistent input rather than re-serializing HTML.

The 'input' listener updates only `valueInternal` and never reflects back to the `value` attribute, so after the user types, `getAttribute('value')` is stale relative to the `.value` property. This attribute/property drift is inconsistent given `value` is in observedAttributes and the setter otherwise reflects it.

Very thin attribute surface: no `type` (always hardcoded type="text", so no email/password/number/search), no `name` (and the element is not form-associated via ElementInternals, so it won't participate in native form submission), no `required`, and no `readonly`. observedAttributes is limited to disabled/label/placeholder/value.

The `value` setter calls setAttribute('value', ...) AND render(); setAttribute triggers attributeChangedCallback('value') which sets valueInternal and calls render() again — a redundant double render per programmatic value assignment.

No aria-invalid, no aria-describedby wiring for helper/error text, and no required/aria-required indication. The default label falls back to a generic "Input" when unset rather than requiring an accessible name, so a mis-authored instance silently gets a meaningless label.

No dispatched 'change' event on commit/blur — only a custom 'value-changed' fires on every keystroke. Consumers expecting a standard change-on-blur semantic (and native form ecosystems) get nothing; the nonstandard event name also diverges from a 'box-*'/'change' convention.

Styling deviates from Box Blueprint's flat field aesthetic: the input uses a subtle top-to-bottom gradient background instead of a flat surface, and the label is uppercase + letter-spacing 0.08em + font-weight 700, whereas Blueprint field labels are sentence-case medium-weight. Token references themselves are correct.

No error/invalid state at all. The component accepts min/max/step (which make the native time input capable of :invalid), and the design system exposes SurfaceStatusSurfaceError (--boe-token-surface-status-surface-error #ed3757), yet render() has no [part="input"]:invalid styling, no error attribute, and no error/help message slot. Out-of-range input gives the user no visual feedback.
No :active/pressed state and no distinct focus treatment when the field also fails validation. Only hover, focus-visible, and disabled are implemented (lines 149-162).

No aria-invalid is ever set on the input, so assistive tech is never told the value is out of the min/max range. Combined with the missing error state, validation is completely invisible to screen-reader users.

Not form-associated. The inner <input> has no name attribute and the element is not a form-associated custom element (no static formAssociated, no ElementInternals), so a <box-time-field> inside a <form> contributes nothing on submit — a significant gap for a form control.

Only a non-standard 'value-changed' CustomEvent is dispatched (line 91). No standard 'change' event is emitted, and it fires on every 'input' (keystroke/spinner) rather than also signaling commit, so consumers expecting native-like change semantics get nothing.

render() replaces the entire shadowRoot.innerHTML on every attribute change (line 108). Programmatically setting .value (or changing label/min/max/step) while the input is focused destroys and recreates the <input>, dropping focus and caret position. A time field whose value is updated externally during interaction will yank focus away.

The value setter double-renders: set value calls setAttribute('value', ...) which triggers attributeChangedCallback -> render() (lines 81-87), and then the setter itself also calls render() (line 74). Every programmatic value assignment rebuilds the DOM twice.

State divergence: on user 'input', syncValue() updates valueInternal and fires the event but never reflects to the 'value' attribute (lines 89-98), while the value setter does reflect. After typing, getAttribute('value') is stale relative to the live value, so attribute-based reads/inspection are inconsistent.

Label styling deviates from Box Blueprint: uppercase, font-weight 700, letter-spacing 0.08em (lines 121-127). Blueprint field labels are normal-case, medium weight. The decorative gradient background (lines 137-142) and the arbitrary 0.7rem radius are also non-standard versus Blueprint's flat surfaces and token-driven radii.

summary: A tidy, token-driven native time input with correct attribute reflection, hover/focus/disabled states, and proper HTML escaping, but it lacks any error/invalid state or aria-invalid, is not form-associated (no name, no ElementInternals), and its full-innerHTML re-render drops focus on programmatic value changes. Solid baseline, short of Box production quality.

issues.description: Docs/API mismatch: docs-site/examples.ts renders `<box-toast title="Link copied" message="...">` but observedAttributes is only ["message","open","tone"] and there is no `title` accessor or render slot. The intended two-line design (bold headline 'Link copied' + secondary detail) is silently dropped — only the message line renders, so the documented example does not match the component.

Live-region announcement is unreliable. render() writes the whole `
…message…
` into shadowRoot.innerHTML in one shot, so the live region and its text are inserted into the DOM simultaneously; many screen readers do not announce content that is present when the live region first appears. A persistent, always-present live-region container whose text is updated after insertion is required for consistent announcements.

Auto-dismiss (default 2500ms in show()) has no pause-on-hover/focus and no user control to extend it, violating WCAG 2.2.1 (Timing Adjustable). Error toasts also auto-dismiss on the same timer — error/critical messages should persist or use aria-live="assertive", but the role is hardcoded to status/polite regardless of tone.
Enter/exit is an instant innerHTML swap with no transition/animation — the toast pops in and vanishes with no fade or slide, well below Box production polish for transient notifications.

Text and chrome colors are hardcoded rather than tokenized: `color: #ffffff` (should map to --boe-token-text-text-on-brand), the `box-shadow` rgba, and all dismiss-button colors (border/color use raw rgba(255,255,255,…)). Only the border-left accents are token-driven; radius 0.75rem and paddings are ad-hoc rather than on a Blueprint spacing/radius scale.

Semantic token misuse: tone="warning" is mapped to --boe-token-surface-status-surface-inprogress (the in-progress/amber token). There is no dedicated warning token, so warning and inprogress are visually conflated; the accent should be a warning-specific token or documented as intentional.

The dismiss button implements hover and focus-visible but no active/pressed state, and there is no visual affordance on the toast for the auto-dismiss countdown (e.g. progress bar) that would communicate the timed behavior.

Duration is only configurable via the imperative show(msg,{duration}) options; there is no `duration` attribute/property, so declarative markup cannot control auto-dismiss timing. tone is also unvalidated — any string is accepted and falls back to the brand accent.

summary: A functional, correctly-escaped toast with clean token-based tone accents and imperative show/hide API, but it falls short of production fidelity: the documented `title` attribute is unsupported, the aria-live region is inserted-and-populated at once (unreliable announcements), auto-dismiss has no pause-on-hover, and text/chrome colors plus enter/exit animation are hardcoded or absent.

A functional but skeuomorphic info-tooltip whose implementation contradicts its own documented API: the docs example slots a box-button trigger, but the component renders a hardcoded "?" button with no slot, so slotted content is silently dropped. Full-innerHTML re-render on every state change causes DOM thrashing and event races during interaction.

issues.description: No <slot> element exists. The docs example (<box-tooltip label="Copy link" open><box-button label="Share"></box-button></box-tooltip>) passes a box-button as the trigger, but render() only outputs a hardcoded <button>?</button> and never renders slotted children. The documented composition (attach a tooltip to an arbitrary element) is completely non-functional — the box-button never appears.

Every state change calls render(), which blows away the entire shadow DOM via innerHTML and re-attaches all listeners. Because mouseenter/focus/click handlers themselves call show()/hide() -> render(), the trigger element being interacted with is destroyed and recreated mid-event: focus is lost after a click, blur fires on a detached node, and the click toggle races with the focus->show handler (focus opens it, then the click handler on the now-orphaned old button immediately calls hide()), producing confusing open/close behavior.

The tooltip panel [part="tooltip"] is placed in normal inline-grid flow (gap: 0.5rem) rather than absolutely positioned. Although the container is position:relative, the tooltip is not position:absolute, so opening it reflows and shifts surrounding page layout instead of overlaying. There is also no placement/collision logic (top/bottom/left/right).

Heavy hardcoded, skeuomorphic styling instead of flat Blueprint tokens: tooltip text color is hardcoded rgba(255,255,255,0.94); borders and shadows use literal rgba() values; the trigger uses linear-gradient white-mixing plus an inset white highlight box-shadow (rgba(255,255,255,0.78)). Only surface/stroke/brand tokens are tokenized — text, focus, and elevation values bypass --boe-token-* variables.

Redundant labeling: the trigger carries aria-label equal to `label`, and the tooltip element (role="tooltip") also contains the same `label` text referenced via aria-describedby. When open, screen readers announce the same string twice (as the button's name and as its description). The trigger content is a bare "?" glyph, so its accessible name is entirely the tooltip text rather than a distinct action label.

Escape-to-dismiss only works while the trigger button has focus (keydown listener is on the trigger, and blur immediately calls hide() anyway). There is no document-level or container-level key handling, and the tooltip cannot be hovered into (mouseleave on the trigger hides it), limiting keyboard/pointer robustness.

State-change events are inconsistent: the open setter dispatches the 'open-changed' CustomEvent, but attributeChangedCallback syncs openValue directly without dispatching, so toggling the `open` attribute externally updates state and re-renders but never emits open-changed. The show()/hide() methods route through the setter, but attribute-driven changes do not.

Instance id uses Math.random().toString(36).slice(2,10) rather than a collision-resistant source; low risk but unnecessary given the component already re-renders id-dependent aria wiring on every change.

summary: A reasonably complete single-select tree with roving-tabindex keyboard nav, brand-blue theming and a selected state, but it is undermined by real correctness bugs (label-based key collisions, an expanded-state Set that never resets), a full innerHTML re-render on every keystroke, and light-mode-only visuals that won't survive the supported dark theme.

issues.description: Node identity is keyed by `item.value ?? item.label` (renderItems L171, getVisibleNodes L134, seedExpandedState L101). Two items sharing a label with no value — or a value that equals another node's label — collide in `expandedInternal` and in keyboard `findIndex(node => node.key === key)` (L500), so expand/collapse and arrow-key focus land on the wrong node. A tree must key on a stable unique id/path, not a display label.

`expandedInternal` is never cleared. `seedExpandedState` (L98) only ever adds keys, and `attributeChangedCallback`/the `items` setter call it again on every items change (L68, L88) without resetting. Swapping the `items` data leaves stale expansion keys accumulating in the Set forever, so collapse-all disable logic and expansion state drift out of sync with the actual tree.

`seedExpandedState` guards with `depth === 0` (L100) yet still recurses into children with `depth + 1` (L103); because the guard fails at depth 1+, that recursion can never add anything. The recursive call is dead code and the intent (auto-expand only the top level) is expressed confusingly.

The render template has an unbalanced trailing `` (L440–441 close `shell` and then emit a second stray `` after the tree). The browser silently repairs it in innerHTML, but it is a real markup bug indicating the shell structure isn't what the author intended.

Every interaction — select, toggle, and each arrow/Home/End keypress — calls `this.render()` which blows away and rebuilds the entire shadow DOM and re-attaches all listeners (L222–551), then restores focus via a `queueMicrotask` hack (L553). This is brittle and O(n) per keystroke; it also causes a visible focus round-trip and would not scale to large trees.

Visuals are hardcoded for light mode: white glossy gradients and `box-shadow: inset 0 1px 0 rgba(255,255,255,0.7x)` (L304, L325, L406) plus `white` mix stops are baked in. The design system ships a supported box-dark theme that re-maps the same token keys, but these literal-white highlights and gradients do not adapt and will look wrong on dark surfaces.

Several referenced tokens do not exist in the token set, so the component silently always uses hardcoded fallbacks: `--boe-token-surface-item-surface-hover` (#eef4fb, L390–391) and `--boe-token-text-text-secondary` are used, but box-defaults defines only `SurfaceItemSurfaceSelected` and `TextTextSecondary` (i.e. `--boe-token-text-text-secondary` is fine, but there is no item-surface-hover token). The hover color is effectively a hardcoded magic value, not a token.

Radii and spacing are arbitrary magic values not drawn from a token/scale: border-radius jumps between 0.9rem, 0.85rem, 0.375rem, 0.35rem and padding uses 0.16rem/0.46rem/0.68rem/0.55rem (L271–366). This breaks Blueprint's radius/spacing rhythm and reads as ad-hoc rather than systematized.

No `:active`/pressed state is defined for items, toggles, or the expand/collapse controls, and there is no hover feedback on the toggle or control buttons (only `:focus-visible`, L409). Disabled styling exists only on the expand/collapse-all controls (via the `disabled` attribute); tree items and toggles have no disabled affordance.

The ARIA tree structure is broken: the `role="group"` container holding children is a sibling of the `treeitem` button inside `part="node"` (L179 vs L184), so it is not a DOM descendant of the element that carries `aria-expanded`. Per the WAI-ARIA tree pattern the group must be owned by/inside the treeitem; here the expansion relationship is not programmatically conveyed. Additionally `aria-expanded` is duplicated on both the toggle button and the treeitem button (L192 vs L204), which is redundant/confusing for assistive tech.

No type-ahead (typing a character to jump to the next matching node) is implemented, which the tree pattern expects. `aria-selected` is only emitted for leaf nodes that have a `value` (L205), so branch selection state is not exposed, and the container never sets `aria-multiselectable` to clarify single-select semantics.

Expansion changes dispatch no event — `toggleExpanded`, `expandAll`, `collapseAll` (L108–125) mutate state and re-render but emit nothing, so consumers can't observe expand/collapse. Only leaf selection fires `value-changed` (L485). A tree should also surface an expand/collapse (or toggle) event.

The `value` setter sets `valueInternal` then calls `setAttribute("value", ...)` which triggers `attributeChangedCallback` (L75–79, L91–95); both paths call `render()`, so a single programmatic value change renders twice. Harmless but wasteful and indicates the reflect-vs-internal flow isn't deduplicated.

summary: A structurally ARIA-competent treegrid that ships almost entirely unstyled: only the two expand/collapse control buttons have CSS, while the grid body, rows, cells, indentation, and selection have no internal styles and the grid-layout custom properties are dead. The documented example also uses an incompatible data shape that throws at render.

issues.description: The shadow-root <style> block (lines 276-321) only styles [part="controls"] and [part~="control"]. Nothing styles the actual grid: [part="tree-grid"], header-row, header-cell, row, tree-cell, cell, tree-content, toggle, item, row-selected/item-selected, spacer, or empty. Out of the box the component renders as unstyled stacked divs with no borders, spacing, rhythm, or Box Blueprint polish.

The section sets style="--tree-grid-columns:${columnTemplate}" (line 342) and each tree-content sets --tree-grid-depth (line 222), but no CSS rule ever consumes them (no grid-template-columns:var(--tree-grid-columns) and no padding-inline-start based on --tree-grid-depth). Result: columns do not lay out as a grid and tree nesting shows no indentation — the core visual structure of a tree grid is missing.

The docs-site example (examples.ts:162-177) is incompatible with the component's API. It passes items as { id, cells:{name,owner}, children } but the component's BoxTreeGridItem requires { value, label, cells:string[] }. At render escapeHtml(item.label) calls .replaceAll on undefined (throws), and (item.cells ?? []).map treats an object as an array (throws). The documented example crashes on load.

No visual state styling for the interactive tree. The keyboard-focusable rowheader item buttons and toggle buttons have no :focus-visible ring (focus-visible is styled only for controls, line 317), no hover, and no active/pressed feedback. Selection sets data-selected/aria-selected/part="*-selected" but nothing renders a selected background or Box-blue accent.

The expand-all/collapse-all controls receive the disabled attribute (lines 330, 338) but there is no disabled CSS (opacity/cursor:not-allowed), so a disabled control is visually indistinguishable from an enabled one.

Only a single event is dispatched: 'value-changed' on selection (lines 382-388). Expansion changes — toggleExpanded, expandAll, collapseAll, and keyboard Arrow expand/collapse — dispatch no events, so consumers cannot observe or persist expand/collapse state. A treegrid should emit expand/collapse (or toggle) events.

Every keystroke and toggle calls render(), which blows away shadowRoot.innerHTML and rebuilds the whole tree, then refocuses via queueMicrotask (lines 450-457). focusValue is never cleared, so after keyboard-navigating and then clicking a different item, the subsequent value-driven re-render steals focus back to the last focusValue row. Focus management is brittle and full re-render is costly for large trees.

role="rowheader" is placed on a <button> (line 236), overriding the implicit button role so assistive tech no longer announces it as an activatable button. ArrowRight/ArrowLeft only expand/collapse and never move focus into the first child or out to the parent as WAI-ARIA treegrid expects, and the focusable item has no visible focus indicator.

seedExpandedState recurses into children with depth+1 but the guard `depth === 0` means the recursion can never satisfy the condition (line 123), so the recursive call at line 125 does nothing useful — dead recursion that only expands top-level branches.

summary: A well-architected share modal with strong dialog semantics, a headless controller, in-flight guarding, and focus-preserving in-place patching. Fidelity and interaction polish fall short of production: no hover/active states despite tokens existing, a non-existent error token, and color-mix against hardcoded white that breaks in dark mode.

issues.description: No :hover or :active styles exist on any interactive control (Copy, Done, Invite, close, tabs). The design system defines SurfaceSurfaceBrandHover (#0057c0) and SurfaceSurfaceBrandPressed (#004eaa) tokens specifically for this, but they are never used. Buttons give no pointer feedback, which reads as unfinished for a primary-action modal.

The error text uses var(--boe-token-text-text-danger, #b3261e) but no TextTextDanger token exists in box-defaults.ts, so the fallback ALWAYS applies — and #b3261e is a Material-design red, not Box's actual error color #ed3757 (SurfaceStatusSurfaceError). Error text renders off-brand in every theme.

Several backgrounds use color-mix(... white NN%) with a literal white: the tablist background (surface-secondary 70%, white 30%) and the avatar background (surface-brand 16%, white 84%). In dark mode the surrounding tokens flip dark but these mixes still pull toward hardcoded white, producing washed-out / inverted contrast. Should mix against a surface token, not white.

No focus restoration: close() sets open=false and clears the shadow DOM but never returns focus to the element that opened the modal (e.g. the Share button). Keyboard/AT users are dropped to document start after dismissal — a core modal contract that trapFocus/Escape otherwise imply is handled.

The error paragraph (part="error") has no role="alert" or aria-live, and the loading→error/ready body swaps are silent. A screen-reader user gets no announcement when a load or setAccess fails, nor when the shared link finishes loading.

The updatingLink busy state has no visible affordance. patchLinkTab sets aria-busy on the <select> but nothing dims, disables, or shows a spinner, so a slow access change looks frozen. Copy also has no success/pressed feedback even though linkcopied fires.

Copy success is not announced. copyLink dispatches the linkcopied event but there is no in-modal aria-live confirmation, so keyboard/AT users get no feedback that the URL was copied (the host toast is external and not guaranteed).
The backdrop scrim rgba(15,23,42,0.45) and the dialog/tab box-shadows are hardcoded cool-slate values rather than tokens, slightly off the warm-neutral Blueprint palette and not theme-aware.

The controller emits linkChanged when access changes, but the element never re-dispatches it as a DOM event. Hosts can observe close/invite/linkcopied but have no DOM signal for shared-link access changes without wiring into the controller directly.
No body scroll lock while open (:host uses display:contents and nothing sets overflow:hidden on the document), so the page behind the aria-modal dialog can still scroll on wheel/trackpad.
